moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: What does this module say about some patterns at the code level?
optionA: All patterns look unique in code
optionB: Some patterns look almost identical in structure
optionC: Patterns never share code shapes
correctAnswerText: Some patterns look almost identical in structure
rationale: Some patterns share nearly identical structure (e.g., Builder vs Method Chaining), so ambiguity is inherent — not a defect.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Why do Builder and Method Chaining look similar in code?
optionB: Both rely on inheritance
optionC: Both use fluent method chaining (return *this)
correctAnswerText: Both use fluent method chaining (return *this)
rationale: Both express a fluent interface returning *this, so their surface code looks alike though their intent differs.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Two classes each hold a member of the same interface and forward calls to it. To tell which pattern, you should examine ___.
optionB: the intent — e.g. controlling access vs adding behaviour vs adapting an interface
optionC: only the variable names
correctAnswerText: the intent — e.g. controlling access vs adding behaviour vs adapting an interface
rationale: Wrappers that forward to a same-interface member are distinguished by intent (control access vs add behaviour vs adapt), not by size, names, or compiler output.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy all wrap a held member. Which cue best separates Proxy from Decorator?
optionA: Proxy adds new responsibilities while Decorator restricts access
optionB: They cannot be distinguished
optionC: Proxy controls access to a same-interface subject; Decorator augments behaviour through the same interface
correctAnswerText: Proxy controls access to a same-interface subject; Decorator augments behaviour through the same interface
rationale: Proxy’s intent is controlling access to the subject (same interface); Decorator’s intent is augmenting behaviour. That intent difference is the cue.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Given two near-identical implementations, which is the most defensible way to resolve the pattern?
optionB: Always choose Decorator
optionC: Flip a coin
optionD: Resolve by the intent shown in naming, collaborators, and call-site shape
correctAnswerText: Resolve by the intent shown in naming, collaborators, and call-site shape
rationale: The defensible method weighs intent evidence (lexemes, collaborators, call-site shape) rather than guessing or defaulting.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Which statement reflects this module’s honest claim?
optionA: Structural ambiguity between some patterns is inherent
optionB: Every pattern is structurally unique
optionC: Ambiguity means the code is wrong
correctAnswerText: Structural ambiguity between some patterns is inherent
rationale: The module’s claim is that structural ambiguity between some patterns is inherent, not a sign of error.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Why can two patterns share the same class diagram?
optionB: Because structure alone does not capture intent, which differs
optionC: Because they are the same pattern
correctAnswerText: Because structure alone does not capture intent, which differs
rationale: A class diagram shows structure but not intent; two patterns with the same structure differ in purpose.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: A class implements an interface, holds a same-interface member, forwards calls, and adds logging around them. Which intent is most likely?
optionB: Ensuring a single instance
optionC: Adding behaviour transparently (decoration)
correctAnswerText: Adding behaviour transparently (decoration)
rationale: Forwarding through the same interface while adding behaviour (logging) signals Decorator intent, not adaptation, singleton, or factory.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: What evidence best distinguishes Adapter from Decorator?
optionB: Adapter targets a different/incompatible interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and adds behaviour
optionC: Decorator changes the interface
correctAnswerText: Adapter targets a different/incompatible interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and adds behaviour
rationale: Adapter converts to a different/incompatible interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and augments behaviour — the defining difference.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Which approach to resolving a near-tie between two patterns is most academically defensible?
optionB: Always pick the first catalog match
optionC: Ignore the tie entirely
optionD: Weigh intent signals and accept genuine ambiguity rather than forcing a single label
correctAnswerText: Weigh intent signals and accept genuine ambiguity rather than forcing a single label
rationale: Weighing intent signals and acknowledging genuine ambiguity is defensible; guessing, defaulting, or ignoring the tie is not.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: What does this module say about some patterns sharing the same code shape?
optionA: It is a bug in the catalog that must be fixed
optionB: It is a built-in property — different patterns can share a structural shape
optionC: It never happens in practice
correctAnswerText: It is a built-in property — different patterns can share a structural shape
rationale: Shared structure across patterns is an inherent property, not a defect — e.g. Builder and Method Chaining both use return *this.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Why can no automated tool always pick a single pattern from structure alone?
optionB: Because the patterns are secret
optionC: Because several patterns can share an identical structural shape, so structure alone is insufficient to decide intent
correctAnswerText: Because several patterns can share an identical structural shape, so structure alone is insufficient to decide intent
rationale: When patterns share a shape, structure underdetermines intent, so a tool cannot always pick one winner from code alone.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Your analyser finds code matching both Adapter and Decorator equally. Following the module, what is the right response?
optionA: Randomly pick one and report it confidently
optionB: Always choose the alphabetically first pattern
optionC: Delete the code
optionD: Report the genuine ambiguity rather than bluffing a single confident answer
correctAnswerText: Report the genuine ambiguity rather than bluffing a single confident answer
rationale: The honest response to a true structural tie is to surface the ambiguity, not to fake a confident single pick.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: Two classes each wrap a held member and forward calls to it. Why is this evidence insufficient to label one Adapter and the other Decorator?
optionA: Because forwarding is illegal in C++
optionB: Because wrapping always means Adapter
optionC: Because Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy all share that wrap-and-forward shape; intent (convert vs add vs control) must distinguish them
optionD: Because the classes must be merged
correctAnswerText: Because Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy all share that wrap-and-forward shape; intent (convert vs add vs control) must distinguish them
rationale: Wrap-and-forward is common to Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy; only their differing intent separates them, so structure alone cannot decide.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

moduleTitle: Ambiguity is built in to design patterns
prompt: A tool reports “100% Decorator” for code that is structurally identical to Adapter and Proxy, with no extra evidence. How should you judge this report?
optionA: Trustworthy — high confidence means it is correct
optionB: Correct because the number is high
optionC: Correct because Decorator is most common
optionD: Unjustified — with a shared shape and no disambiguating evidence, a single high-confidence claim is not defensible
correctAnswerText: Unjustified — with a shared shape and no disambiguating evidence, a single high-confidence claim is not defensible
rationale: A confident single label on a genuinely ambiguous shape, lacking disambiguating evidence, is not defensible — the module’s core lesson.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Which is a common beginner mistake with design patterns?
optionB: Forcing a pattern where the underlying problem does not exist
optionC: Writing unit tests
correctAnswerText: Forcing a pattern where the underlying problem does not exist
rationale: Over-applying a pattern where its problem is absent is a classic trap; the other options are good practices.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Why is naming a class after a pattern not evidence the pattern is implemented?
optionB: Because the pattern must be in a separate file
optionC: Because a name is only a label; structure and intent determine the pattern
correctAnswerText: Because a name is only a label; structure and intent determine the pattern
rationale: A name is just a label; the pattern exists only if the structure/intent are present. The other options are incorrect.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Over-applying design patterns most often leads to ___.
optionB: fewer source files
optionC: guaranteed correctness
correctAnswerText: unnecessary complexity and indirection
rationale: Forcing patterns adds indirection/complexity without benefit; it does not improve speed, file count, or correctness.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: A learner adds “Factory” to a class name to “use the pattern.” What is the flaw?
optionB: Renaming does not create the pattern’s structure or intent
optionC: Factories must be singletons
optionD: Patterns cannot be named at all
correctAnswerText: Renaming does not create the pattern’s structure or intent
rationale: Renaming alone does not create the required structure/intent, so the pattern is not implemented. The other options are wrong.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Which is a common beginner mistake this module warns against?
optionA: Reading the existing code before changing it
optionB: Naming variables clearly
optionC: Forcing a pattern where a simple solution would do (over-engineering)
correctAnswerText: Forcing a pattern where a simple solution would do (over-engineering)
rationale: Over-applying patterns where a simple solution suffices is a classic beginner trap the module warns against.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Why is naming a class “FactoryManager” NOT enough to claim it uses a pattern?
optionA: Because class names must be lowercase
optionB: Because patterns cannot be used in named classes
optionC: Because the compiler ignores names
optionD: Because a pattern is defined by its structure and intent, not by a label in the name
correctAnswerText: Because a pattern is defined by its structure and intent, not by a label in the name
rationale: A pattern is identified by structure and intent; a suggestive class name alone is not evidence the pattern is present.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Which trio names the three classic Gang-of-Four pattern families?
optionB: Frontend, Backend, Database
optionC: Creational, Structural, Behavioural
correctAnswerText: Creational, Structural, Behavioural
rationale: The GoF families are Creational, Structural, and Behavioural. The other groupings are build phases, app tiers, and binding kinds — not pattern families.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A pattern’s main job is deciding HOW objects are created. Which family is it in?
optionB: Structural
optionC: Behavioural
optionD: None — creation is never a pattern concern
correctAnswerText: Creational
rationale: Creational patterns address object creation/instantiation concerns. Structural deals with composition; Behavioural with communication; creation is squarely a Creational concern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: Which sentence correctly maps the three families by intent?
optionA: Creational connects objects; Structural decides communication; Behavioural makes objects
optionB: Creational makes objects; Structural composes/connects them; Behavioural decides how they communicate
optionC: All three families do the same thing under different names
optionD: Structural is a special case of Creational
correctAnswerText: Creational makes objects; Structural composes/connects them; Behavioural decides how they communicate
rationale: Creational = how objects are made; Structural = how they are composed/related; Behavioural = how responsibilities/communication are organised. The other options swap or conflate these intents.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: The Gang-of-Four catalogue groups patterns into three families. Which option lists them?
optionB: Public, Private, Protected
optionC: Stack, Heap, Register
correctAnswerText: Behavioural, Structural, Creational
rationale: The three GoF families are Creational, Structural, and Behavioural (order aside). Access specifiers, memory regions, and MVC are unrelated groupings.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A pattern primarily organises how responsibilities are assigned and how objects talk at runtime. Which family?
optionB: Structural
optionC: Behavioural
optionD: None — communication is not a pattern concern
correctAnswerText: Behavioural
rationale: Behavioural patterns concern responsibility assignment and runtime communication between objects. Creational is about making objects; Structural about composing them.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: Which mapping of family-to-intent is correct?
optionA: Structural makes objects; Behavioural composes them; Creational handles communication
optionB: All families address object creation only
optionC: Behavioural is a subset of Structural
optionD: Creational = object creation; Structural = composition/relationships; Behavioural = communication/responsibility
correctAnswerText: Creational = object creation; Structural = composition/relationships; Behavioural = communication/responsibility
rationale: Only the last option maps each family to its real intent. The others swap intents, over-narrow to creation, or invent a subset relationship.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: What are the three main GoF pattern categories described in this module?
optionB: Easy, moderate, hard
optionC: Compile, link, run
correctAnswerText: Creational, Structural, Behavioural
rationale: The three GoF categories are Creational, Structural, and Behavioural; the module adds a fourth bucket of C++ idioms.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: On what basis are patterns grouped into those categories?
optionA: By how many lines of code they need
optionB: By the year they were invented
optionC: By what the pattern is responsible for — creating objects, organising them, or coordinating behaviour
correctAnswerText: By what the pattern is responsible for — creating objects, organising them, or coordinating behaviour
rationale: Categories reflect responsibility: object creation (Creational), structure/composition (Structural), and communication/behaviour (Behavioural).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: What does CodiNeo’s analyser inspect to detect patterns?

correctAnswerText: Class relationships and structure via a parse tree (AST)

correctAnswerText: Because patterns are defined by relationships and responsibilities, not identifiers

prompt: Which representation lets a tool analyse class relationships and dependencies?

prompt: A class is named “ObserverManager” but never notifies dependents. Why won’t a structure-based analyser tag it as Observer?
optionB: Because Observer must live in its own file

correctAnswerText: Because detection relies on structural evidence, not the class name

prompt: What does AST stand for in this module?

prompt: Why can patterns be detected as “shapes” in code structure rather than by reading comments?

correctAnswerText: Because class relationships, inheritance, and call flow form recognisable structural arrangements a tool can analyse

prompt: According to this module, the way to reduce pattern ambiguity is to ___.

correctAnswerText: A pattern’s meaning sharpens when intent/context is added to its bare structure

prompt: To reduce ambiguity, the connotative-definition rule says you should ___.

correctAnswerText: add contextual intent to the definition rather than guess

correctAnswerText: By enriching a pattern’s structural definition with intent/context cues

prompt: The connotative-definition rule reduces ambiguity by ___.

optionC: removing all definitions
correctAnswerText: adding descriptive context to the definition so the meaning narrows

moduleTitle: Patterns vary by team and codebase
prompt: How does CodiNeo keep detection consistent despite team-by-team variation?

optionC: By refusing to analyse any code

prompt: What does “program to an interface, not an implementation” mean?
optionA: Always use abstract classes for everything
optionB: Write all code in header files
optionC: Depend on a shared abstraction instead of one specific concrete class
optionD: Avoid interfaces to reduce the file count
correctAnswerText: Depend on a shared abstraction instead of one specific concrete class
rationale: The principle says to depend on a shared abstraction (capability) rather than a specific concrete class. It is not about abstract classes everywhere, header files, or avoiding interfaces.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: Why does depending on an abstraction make a system easier to change?
optionA: It always reduces the number of classes
optionB: Callers are decoupled from concrete types, so implementations can be swapped without editing callers
optionC: It makes the program run faster
optionD: It removes the need for tests
correctAnswerText: Callers are decoupled from concrete types, so implementations can be swapped without editing callers
rationale: Decoupling callers from concrete types lets implementations be substituted without changing the callers. It does not inherently reduce classes, improve speed, or remove tests.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: A team must support several payment providers and switch between them without changing checkout code. Which approach applies the principle?
optionA: Inline every provider into the checkout code
optionB: Store the active provider in a global variable
optionC: Hard-code one default provider
optionD: Have checkout depend on a payment abstraction that each provider implements
correctAnswerText: Have checkout depend on a payment abstraction that each provider implements
rationale: Depending on a payment abstraction implemented by each provider lets checkout stay unchanged while providers vary — the principle in action. The other options couple checkout to concretes.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: “Program to an interface” tells you to depend on ___.
optionA: a shared abstraction rather than a specific concrete class
optionB: the most-derived class available
optionC: only static free functions
correctAnswerText: a shared abstraction rather than a specific concrete class
rationale: The rule is to depend on a shared abstraction rather than a specific concrete class. The other options misstate it.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A module depends on an abstraction rather than a concrete class. What does this most enable?
optionB: Fewer source files in all cases
optionC: The ability to substitute implementations without changing the dependent module
correctAnswerText: The ability to substitute implementations without changing the dependent module
rationale: An abstraction dependency enables substituting implementations without editing the dependent module. It gives no memory, file-count, or threading guarantee.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: A logging component must write to console, file, or network sinks chosen at runtime without changing callers. Which approach applies the principle?
optionA: Duplicate the logger for each sink
optionB: Depend on a sink abstraction that each backend implements
optionC: Put a large switch statement in every caller
optionD: Make every sink a global object
correctAnswerText: Depend on a sink abstraction that each backend implements
rationale: Depending on a sink abstraction implemented by each backend lets the sink vary at runtime without changing callers. The alternatives couple callers to concretes.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: The principle taught in this module is “program to an ___.”
optionB: integer, not a string
optionC: inline function, not a macro
correctAnswerText: interface, not an implementation
rationale: The principle is “program to an interface, not an implementation” — depend on abstractions, not concrete classes.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Why does depending on an abstraction instead of a concrete class make a system easier to change?
optionA: It deletes all the concrete classes
optionB: It forces every class to be final
optionC: It makes the program compile without linking
optionD: New or swapped implementations can be used without modifying the callers that depend only on the abstraction
correctAnswerText: New or swapped implementations can be used without modifying the callers that depend only on the abstraction
rationale: Callers that depend on an abstraction are insulated from concrete changes, so implementations can be added or swapped without editing them.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Several callers each hard-code PayPalProcessor. To follow this module’s principle, what should they depend on instead?
optionA: The exact PayPalProcessor class, but copied into each caller
optionB: A global PayPal instance
optionC: A shared PaymentProcessor abstraction that any concrete processor implements
correctAnswerText: A shared PaymentProcessor abstraction that any concrete processor implements
rationale: Depending on a shared PaymentProcessor abstraction lets any concrete processor be substituted without changing the callers.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Which OOP mechanism lets one interface call be served by different concrete implementations at runtime?
optionB: Polymorphism
optionC: Macro expansion
correctAnswerText: Polymorphism
rationale: Polymorphism (via virtual dispatch) lets a call through a base interface run different concrete behaviour. Encapsulation hides state; macros and linking are unrelated build-time mechanisms.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Why do many design patterns depend on programming to an interface (abstraction) rather than a concrete class?
optionA: It lets implementations vary independently of the code that uses them
optionB: It always reduces the number of classes
optionC: It makes the program use less memory
optionD: It removes the need for constructors
correctAnswerText: It lets implementations vary independently of the code that uses them
rationale: Depending on an abstraction decouples clients from concrete types, so implementations can change/substitute without touching callers — the basis of many patterns. It does not inherently reduce classes/memory or remove constructors.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A base-class pointer invokes a method and the derived class’s version runs. Which OOP concept is this?
optionB: Encapsulation
optionC: Polymorphism
correctAnswerText: Polymorphism
rationale: Dispatching to the derived override through a base pointer is runtime polymorphism. Static binding resolves at compile time; encapsulation and preprocessing are different concepts.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: What does depending on an abstraction (interface) buy a design that depending on a concrete class does not?
optionB: Substitutable implementations without changing client code
optionC: Fewer total source files in every case
correctAnswerText: Substitutable implementations without changing client code
rationale: An interface dependency lets concrete implementations be swapped without editing clients — enabling extension and testing. It gives no memory, file-count, or thread-safety guarantee.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: Which OOP concept means bundling data with the operations that act on it and restricting direct outside access?
optionB: Recursion
optionC: Compilation
correctAnswerText: Encapsulation
rationale: Encapsulation bundles state with behaviour and limits outside access to internals; the others are unrelated.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: Why is polymorphism foundational to most design patterns?
optionB: It forces every method to be static
optionC: It removes the need for classes
optionD: It lets code call a shared interface while different concrete types supply the behaviour
correctAnswerText: It lets code call a shared interface while different concrete types supply the behaviour
rationale: Polymorphism lets callers depend on an abstraction while concrete types vary the behaviour — the basis of Strategy, Factory, Bridge, and more.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content

prompt: After Foundations, what kind of question is the catalog NOT trying to answer?
optionA: Which structural shape this class matches.
optionB: Whether the chosen pattern fits the team’s organisational conventions and goals.

prompt: What is a design pattern, most precisely?

correctAnswerText: A named, reusable solution to a recurring object-oriented design problem
rationale: A design pattern is a named, reusable solution to a recurring OO design problem. It is not a language keyword, a mandatory base class, or a formatting tool.

prompt: A design pattern primarily captures which of the following?

correctAnswerText: A proven structure-and-intent for a recurring design problem

prompt: Why does giving a recurring design a shared name help a team?

prompt: How does a design pattern differ from an algorithm?

prompt: A team keeps re-solving the same object-arrangement problem slightly differently across modules. What would adopting a design pattern give them?

prompt: Which best identifies what the term 'design pattern' refers to?

correctAnswerText: A reusable, named template for solving a common design problem

sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A design pattern is best described as ___.

correctAnswerText: A design pattern — a named, proven solution shape for that recurring problem

correctAnswerText: It lets a recognised name stand in for a paragraph of structural explanation
rationale: Pattern names are a communication tool: one agreed term conveys a known structure and intent, shortening design discussion and review. They do not affect runtime speed or correctness.

prompt: Why does recognising a pattern in existing code help a reviewer?

correctAnswerText: It lets the reviewer infer intent and expected structure quickly, focusing review on real risks
rationale: Recognising intent lets the reviewer reason about responsibilities and collaborations instead of re-deriving them, so attention goes to genuine risks. It says nothing about speed, tests, or language features.

prompt: Which statement best explains a limitation of patterns?

optionD: Patterns make code impossible to refactor later
correctAnswerText: Applying a pattern where it is not needed can add complexity without benefit
rationale: Patterns are solutions to specific recurring problems; forcing one where the problem is absent adds indirection and complexity for no gain. They are not universally correct, are not OO-exclusive, and do not block refactoring.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: Two engineers say “let’s use the Observer here.” What has the pattern name primarily given them?

correctAnswerText: A shared, compact way to communicate a known structure and intent
rationale: The value is communication: the name conveys an agreed structure/intent so the team need not spell it out. It does not change runtime behaviour, remove bugs, or avoid implementation.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: A new hire reads a module and recognises a familiar pattern. How does that most help them?

prompt: When can adopting a design pattern be the wrong choice?

correctAnswerText: When the underlying recurring problem the pattern solves is not actually present
rationale: A pattern earns its complexity only when its target problem exists; applying it speculatively adds indirection without benefit. Team familiarity, inheritance support, and testing are not reasons to avoid it.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Foundations module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns, ch. 1

prompt: According to the module, which is a benefit of using design patterns?

prompt: What is the intent of the Abstract Factory pattern?

correctAnswerText: Provide an interface for creating families of related objects without naming their concrete classes
rationale: Abstract Factory creates families of related products behind one interface, hiding concrete classes. The distractors are Singleton, Prototype, and Decorator.

prompt: How does Abstract Factory differ from Factory Method?

correctAnswerText: It creates whole families of related products, while Factory Method creates one product via subclass override
rationale: Abstract Factory builds families of related products together; Factory Method defers one product’s creation to a subclass. They are not identical.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A UI toolkit must produce matching widgets (button, checkbox, menu) per theme, guaranteed to belong to the same look-and-feel. Which pattern fits?

optionC: Method Chaining

prompt: Examine:  struct GuiFactory { virtual Button* makeButton() = 0; virtual Checkbox* makeCheckbox() = 0; };  Which pattern is this interface part of?

optionC: Abstract Factory
correctAnswerText: Abstract Factory
rationale: One factory interface with multiple create methods for related products (Button + Checkbox) is Abstract Factory; a single create method would be Factory Method.

prompt: A concrete WinFactory implements makeButton() returning a WinButton but makeCheckbox() returns a MacCheckbox. Does this honor Abstract Factory’s intent?

correctAnswerText: No — a concrete factory must return products from one consistent family; mixing Win and Mac breaks the family guarantee
rationale: Abstract Factory’s value is that one concrete factory yields a matching family; returning a Mac checkbox from a Windows factory violates that guarantee.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Abstract Factory is best described as a pattern that ___.

correctAnswerText: creates families of related objects behind one interface, hiding concrete types
rationale: Abstract Factory produces related product families through one interface. The distractors are Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Which statement correctly contrasts Abstract Factory with Factory Method?
optionA: Abstract Factory makes one product; Factory Method makes families

correctAnswerText: Abstract Factory creates a family of related products; Factory Method creates a single product via an overridden method
rationale: Abstract Factory creates coherent families; Factory Method creates one product through a subclass override. The other options invert or conflate them.

prompt: A team replaces two separate makeButton()/makeCheckbox() factory methods with one makeWidget(string kind) switch returning new-ed widgets. Is this still Abstract Factory?

rationale: Abstract Factory relies on a typed interface of related create methods producing a coherent family; a single stringly-typed switch discards that structure.

prompt: What does an Abstract Factory provide through its interface?

correctAnswerText: A set of methods that create a whole matching family of related objects

sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How does Abstract Factory differ from a pattern where a subclass overrides one method to choose a single concrete product?

correctAnswerText: It defines an interface that creates a coordinated family of several related products, not just one product
rationale: Abstract Factory creates a family of related products together; deferring one product’s creation to a subclass override is Factory Method. They are distinct.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A document editor must render either a "Light" or "Dark" theme, and within a chosen theme the toolbar, scrollbar, and tooltip must all match — never a light scrollbar with a dark toolbar. Which approach fits?

correctAnswerText: Provide one interface that creates a matching family of related controls per theme
rationale: Guaranteeing a coherent, non-mixed family of related products per theme is Abstract Factory; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Examine:  struct DbFactory { virtual Connection* connect() = 0; virtual Command* command() = 0; virtual Reader* reader() = 0; };  Which pattern is this interface part of?

rationale: One interface declaring several create methods for related products (Connection, Command, Reader) that a concrete factory implements as a coherent family is Abstract Factory; a single create method would be Factory Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A concrete MySqlFactory implements connect() returning a MySqlConnection but command() returns a PostgresCommand. Does this honor Abstract Factory’s intent?
optionA: Yes — any concrete return types are acceptable
optionB: No — a concrete factory must return products from one consistent family; mixing MySQL and Postgres breaks the family guarantee
optionC: Yes — provided both types compile together
optionD: No — Abstract Factory is limited to a single product method
correctAnswerText: No — a concrete factory must return products from one consistent family; mixing MySQL and Postgres breaks the family guarantee
rationale: The value of Abstract Factory is that one concrete factory yields a matching family; returning a Postgres command from a MySQL factory violates that coherence guarantee.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the primary intent of the Builder pattern?
optionA: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation, so the same process can create different representations
optionB: Ensure a class has only one instance
optionC: Convert one interface into another
correctAnswerText: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation, so the same process can create different representations
rationale: Builder separates constructing a complex object from its representation so one process can yield different representations. The distractors describe Singleton, Adapter, and Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: How does the Builder pattern typically organize object construction?
optionA: By making all fields public and setting them directly
optionB: By using a single giant constructor with many parameters
optionC: Through a sequence of step methods that assemble parts, then a final build step
correctAnswerText: Through a sequence of step methods that assemble parts, then a final build step
rationale: Builder assembles parts via step methods then a final build step, avoiding public mutable fields, telescoping constructors, and cloning.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: A system must assemble configuration objects through a series of optional, ordered steps and produce several different final representations from the same steps. Which pattern best fits?
optionB: Builder
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Builder
rationale: Optional, ordered steps producing different final representations is Builder’s core use case; Singleton, Adapter, and Observer solve unrelated problems.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: A class exposes addPart()/setX() methods that each return the same object, plus a final build() that returns the assembled product; no part is exposed until build(). Which pattern does this structure indicate?
optionB: Facade
optionC: Composite
correctAnswerText: Builder
rationale: Chained part-adding methods returning the same object plus a terminal build() that first exposes the product is Builder’s structural signature.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: An object has many optional parameters and must remain immutable after creation. Which is more appropriate, and why — a step-based assembler that returns a finished product, or a telescoping set of constructors?
optionA: The step-based assembler — it handles optional parameters cleanly and yields an immutable result
optionB: Telescoping constructors — they are always clearer
optionC: Neither; expose public setters instead
correctAnswerText: The step-based assembler — it handles optional parameters cleanly and yields an immutable result
rationale: For many optional parameters plus immutability, a step assembler returning a finished product is clearest and preserves immutability; telescoping constructors and setters do not.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: The Builder pattern is mainly used to ___.
optionB: construct a complex object step by step, allowing different final representations
optionC: wrap an incompatible interface
optionD: define a family of related products
correctAnswerText: construct a complex object step by step, allowing different final representations
rationale: Builder constructs a complex object step by step, allowing different representations. The distractors describe Singleton, Adapter, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: Why does Builder separate a step sequence from the part-assembly?
optionA: To make the object a singleton
optionB: To avoid writing any classes
optionC: To convert interfaces
optionD: So the same construction sequence can yield different representations while construction details stay encapsulated
correctAnswerText: So the same construction sequence can yield different representations while construction details stay encapsulated
rationale: Separating the step sequence from assembly lets one sequence yield different representations while hiding construction details — Builder’s rationale.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: A report generator must build documents through configurable, ordered steps and emit them as PDF or HTML from the same steps. Which pattern is most appropriate?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Builder
rationale: Configurable ordered steps emitting PDF or HTML from one process is Builder; Strategy, Proxy, and Flyweight address different concerns.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: You see a fluent class whose methods return *this to chain calls and a terminal build() that produces the final object only at the end. Which pattern is this structural evidence of?
optionB: Decorator
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Builder
rationale: A fluent class returning *this to chain calls with a terminal build() is structural evidence of Builder; the distractors have different shapes.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: A complex object needs many optional fields and must be validated once, fully assembled. Which approach better meets "assemble incrementally, validate on completion" — a step assembler with a final build, or setting fields directly after construction?
optionB: A step assembler with a final build/validate step
optionC: A global mutable instance
correctAnswerText: A step assembler with a final build/validate step
rationale: Incremental assembly with validation on completion is met by a step assembler with a final build/validate step; direct setters, globals, and cloning do not.
sourceReferences: Gamma et al. (1994), Design Patterns — Builder; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++ — Builder

prompt: In the custom-pizza analogy, what does the Builder ultimately do at the end of the steps?
optionA: Guarantees only one pizza ever exists
optionB: Copies an already-finished pizza
optionC: Produces a matching set of related pizzas
optionD: Returns the finished object after the parts have been set one at a time
correctAnswerText: Returns the finished object after the parts have been set one at a time
rationale: Builder assembles a complex object piece by piece and a terminal step returns the finished result, like saying "done" to get the completed pizza. The distractors are Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Why is Builder preferred over a constructor that takes a long list of arguments for a complex object?
optionB: Because naming each part through separate steps makes the construction readable and lets optional parts be omitted
optionC: Because Builder guarantees the object is a Singleton
optionD: Because Builder removes the need for the object to have any fields
correctAnswerText: Because naming each part through separate steps makes the construction readable and lets optional parts be omitted
rationale: Builder replaces a hard-to-read multi-argument constructor with named steps, so optional knobs stay optional and the intent of each part is clear.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An HTTP request object has a required URL plus many optional pieces (headers, timeout, retries, body). The team wants required parts enforced and optionals set clearly, ending with one finished request. Which approach fits best?
optionA: Assemble it through ordered steps and a terminal finish call
optionB: Make the request a single shared global instance
optionC: Clone a pre-configured request each time
optionD: Provide one factory interface returning a family of related requests
correctAnswerText: Assemble it through ordered steps and a terminal finish call
rationale: Stepwise assembly that enforces required parts and ends with a terminal build is Builder; the distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct HouseBuilder { HouseBuilder& walls(int n); HouseBuilder& roof(std::string t); House build(); };  used as  House h = HouseBuilder{}.walls(4).roof("tile").build();  Which pattern does the presence of the terminal build() most strongly indicate?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Builder (stepwise construction ending in a terminal build)
correctAnswerText: Builder (stepwise construction ending in a terminal build)
rationale: Chained setters that culminate in a terminal build() returning a separate finished product point to Builder; pure Method Chaining configures the same object and lacks the distinct terminal build step.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: A "builder" exposes setters that return *this but has no terminal step and no separate product — callers just use the builder object itself as the final result. Does this honor Builder’s intent?
optionA: Yes — any class with chained setters is a complete Builder
optionB: Yes — a terminal step is forbidden in modern C++
optionC: No — Builder must be a Singleton to be valid
optionD: No — without a terminal step that yields a distinct finished product, this is fluent Method Chaining rather than Builder
correctAnswerText: No — without a terminal step that yields a distinct finished product, this is fluent Method Chaining rather than Builder
rationale: Builder’s intent is to assemble and then hand back a separate finished object via a terminal step; chained setters with no terminal product are Method Chaining, the closely related but distinct idiom.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: What is the intent of the Factory Method pattern?
optionA: Define an interface for creating an object but let subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate
optionB: Ensure a class has a single instance
optionC: Copy an existing object
correctAnswerText: Define an interface for creating an object but let subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate
rationale: Factory Method defers the choice of concrete product to subclasses via an overridable creation method. The distractors describe Singleton, Prototype, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: In Factory Method, who decides which concrete product is created?
optionB: The compiler
optionC: A subclass that overrides the creation method
correctAnswerText: A subclass that overrides the creation method
rationale: The creator declares a creation method; a subclass overrides it to choose the concrete product, decoupling clients from concretes.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A framework defines a workflow but must let each plug-in decide which concrete document object the workflow creates. Which pattern fits?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Method Chaining
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: Letting subclasses/plug-ins choose the concrete product while the base defines the workflow is Factory Method; the distractors solve other problems.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct Creator { virtual Product* create() = 0; void run(){ auto* p = create(); /* use p */ } };  struct PdfCreator : Creator { Product* create() override { return new Pdf(); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Abstract Factory
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: A base class with an overridable create() that subclasses implement to pick the concrete product is Factory Method (one product per call), not a family factory or step builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A class named ShapeFactory contains one giant switch on a string to new each shape, with no polymorphic creator. Is this Factory Method?
optionA: Yes — the name ShapeFactory makes it Factory Method
optionB: Yes — any creation code is Factory Method
optionC: No — Factory Method requires templates
optionD: No — the name does not matter; without a polymorphic creation method overridden by subclasses it is a simple/switch factory, not Factory Method
correctAnswerText: No — the name does not matter; without a polymorphic creation method overridden by subclasses it is a simple/switch factory, not Factory Method
rationale: Factory Method’s defining structure is a creation method overridden by subclasses; a string switch is a simple factory. The class name is not evidence of the pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: Factory Method is best described as a pattern that ___.
optionB: produces whole families of related products
optionC: lets subclasses decide which concrete class an overridable method instantiates
optionD: adds behaviour around an object transparently
correctAnswerText: lets subclasses decide which concrete class an overridable method instantiates
rationale: Factory Method delegates the concrete-product choice to subclasses through an overridable method. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: How does Factory Method decouple a creator from the concrete products it uses?
optionA: By making the product a global variable
optionB: By hard-coding the product type in the base class
optionC: By cloning a prototype instance
optionD: By calling an overridable creation method, so subclasses supply the concrete type
correctAnswerText: By calling an overridable creation method, so subclasses supply the concrete type
rationale: The creator calls its own overridable creation method; subclasses decide the concrete type, so the creator never names a concrete product.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A base dialog class runs the same open/close flow but each dialog kind must instantiate its own button type. Which pattern is most appropriate?
optionB: Factory Method
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: A shared flow whose concrete sub-object is chosen by subclasses is Factory Method; the distractors address single-instance, interface conversion, and copying.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct ShapeCreator { virtual Shape* make() = 0; };  struct CircleCreator : ShapeCreator { Shape* make() override { return new Circle(); } };  Which pattern is this structural evidence of?
optionB: Builder
optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: A virtual make() overridden by a subclass to return one concrete product is Factory Method; it produces a single product, not a family or a step-built object.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A reviewer sees a creator base class with create() overridden by two subclasses to return different products. Is calling this Factory Method defensible?
optionA: No — only a string switch counts as Factory Method
optionB: No — it must also be a Singleton
optionC: Yes — an overridable creation method specialised by subclasses to choose the concrete product is exactly Factory Method
optionD: Yes — but only if the class is named Factory
correctAnswerText: Yes — an overridable creation method specialised by subclasses to choose the concrete product is exactly Factory Method
rationale: Subclasses overriding a creation method to pick the concrete product is the defining structure of Factory Method; the name and Singleton status are irrelevant.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In Factory Method, which participant decides which concrete product is actually created?
optionA: The client code, by naming the concrete class directly
optionB: A subclass that overrides the creation method
optionC: A global registry shared by all callers
optionD: The destructor of the base product
correctAnswerText: A subclass that overrides the creation method
rationale: The base creator declares a creation method; a subclass overrides it to choose the concrete product. The other options bypass the subclass-driven choice that defines the pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How does Factory Method differ from a factory that produces a whole matching set of related objects?
optionA: It cannot use inheritance at all
optionB: It always returns a single shared instance
optionC: It is identical — both create families
optionD: It defers the choice of one product to a subclass override, rather than creating a coordinated family of products
correctAnswerText: It defers the choice of one product to a subclass override, rather than creating a coordinated family of products
rationale: Factory Method picks one product through a subclass-overridden method; producing a coordinated family of related products is Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A report engine runs an identical generate-and-export sequence, but each report kind must construct its own exporter object. The base class wants to stay unaware of which concrete exporter is used. Which approach fits?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Factory Method
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: A fixed flow whose single sub-object is chosen by subclasses through an overridable creation method is Factory Method; the distractors solve single-instance, copying, and fluent configuration.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct Parser { virtual Token* lex() = 0; void run(){ Token* t = lex(); /* ... */ } };  struct JsonParser : Parser { Token* lex() override { return new JsonToken(); } };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
optionB: Abstract Factory
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Factory Method
rationale: A base class calls its own overridable creation method while a subclass implements it to return one concrete product — Factory Method. There is no family, copy, or stepwise build.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A reviewer finds a base Importer class with a virtual openSource() that two subclasses override to return different concrete sources, used by a shared import() routine. Is labeling this Factory Method defensible?
optionA: No — it must use a string switch to qualify
optionB: Yes — a creation method overridden by subclasses to choose the concrete product is exactly Factory Method
optionC: No — it is only Factory Method if the class is named Factory
optionD: Yes — but only if it is also a Singleton
correctAnswerText: Yes — a creation method overridden by subclasses to choose the concrete product is exactly Factory Method
rationale: Subclasses overriding a creation method to select the concrete product is the defining structure of Factory Method; the class name and instance count are irrelevant.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What does the Method Chaining idiom let you do?
optionA: Set several options on the same object in one fluent expression
optionB: Guarantee one instance
optionC: Create matching product families
correctAnswerText: Set several options on the same object in one fluent expression
rationale: Method Chaining returns the same object from each setter so calls can be chained fluently. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: What must a setter return to enable method chaining in C++?
optionB: a new copy of the object
optionC: a reference (or pointer) to the same object, e.g. return *this
correctAnswerText: a reference (or pointer) to the same object, e.g. return *this
rationale: Returning *this (a reference to the same object) lets the next call chain on it. Returning void, a copy, or a flag breaks the chain.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A configuration object should be set up in one readable expression like cfg.host("x").port(80).tls(true);. Which technique enables this?
optionB: Making the class a Singleton
optionC: Cloning the object per setter
optionD: Having each setter return a reference to the object so calls chain
correctAnswerText: Having each setter return a reference to the object so calls chain
rationale: Each setter returning a self-reference makes the fluent chain possible. The other options prevent chaining or solve unrelated problems.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: Examine:  struct Pizza { Pizza& size(int s){ s_=s; return *this; } Pizza& cheese(bool c){ c_=c; return *this; } };  What idiom does this show?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Singleton
correctAnswerText: Method Chaining (fluent interface)
rationale: Setters that mutate and return *this form a fluent interface (Method Chaining). No copying, single-instance, or family creation is present.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A “fluent” API returns a brand-new copy of the object from every setter instead of *this. Does this correctly implement method chaining, and what is the effect?
optionA: Yes — it is identical to returning *this
optionB: Yes — copies are required for chaining
optionC: No — chaining is impossible in C++
optionD: No — returning copies means earlier settings may be lost and each call mutates a different object, defeating the fluent intent
correctAnswerText: No — returning copies means earlier settings may be lost and each call mutates a different object, defeating the fluent intent
rationale: Returning fresh copies means each call operates on a different object, so accumulated configuration can be lost; chaining requires returning the same object (*this).
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: Method Chaining is mainly used to ___.
optionB: produce related product families
optionC: configure one object through a fluent sequence of calls
correctAnswerText: configure one object through a fluent sequence of calls
rationale: Method Chaining configures the same object via a fluent call sequence. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Why does each chained method return *this?
optionB: To enforce a single instance
optionC: To convert the return type
optionD: So the following call can operate on the same object, continuing the chain
correctAnswerText: So the following call can operate on the same object, continuing the chain
rationale: Returning *this hands the same object to the next call, sustaining the fluent chain. The other options are unrelated.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A query builder should read like q.select("*").from("t").where("id=1");. What enables this fluent style?
optionB: Each method returns a reference to the builder so calls chain
optionC: The builder is cloned each call
correctAnswerText: Each method returns a reference to the builder so calls chain
rationale: Each method returning a reference to the builder enables chaining; void, cloning, or Singleton would not.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: Examine:  Query& Query::where(std::string c){ clauses_.push_back(c); return *this; }  // similar for select(), from()  Which idiom is this?
optionB: Singleton
optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Method Chaining (fluent interface)
rationale: Mutating member functions that return *this form a fluent (method-chaining) interface; there is no single-instance, copy, or factory structure.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A setter in a “fluent” class returns void but the author still writes a.x().y();. Is the chaining implemented correctly?
optionA: Yes — void return is fine for chaining
optionB: Yes — provided the methods are const
optionC: No — with a void return the second call has no object to act on, so it will not compile/chain
correctAnswerText: No — with a void return the second call has no object to act on, so it will not compile/chain
rationale: Chaining needs each call to yield the object; a void return leaves nothing to call the next method on, so the chain cannot work.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What does the Method Chaining idiom let you do in a single expression?
optionA: Set several options on the same object one after another
optionB: Guarantee that only one instance of the object exists
optionC: Create a matching family of related objects
correctAnswerText: Set several options on the same object one after another
rationale: Method Chaining returns the same object from each call so options can be set in one fluent expression. The distractors are Singleton, Abstract Factory, and Prototype.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Method Chaining and Builder both rely on returning *this. What distinguishes Method Chaining from Builder?
optionA: Method Chaining must be a Singleton
optionB: Method Chaining clones the object on each call
optionC: Method Chaining configures the same object fluently and has no separate terminal step that hands back a distinct finished product
optionD: Method Chaining can only set one option total
correctAnswerText: Method Chaining configures the same object fluently and has no separate terminal step that hands back a distinct finished product
rationale: Both share the return-*this shape, but Method Chaining simply configures one object fluently, whereas Builder adds a terminal step yielding a separate finished product.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: A developer wants to write log.level(Warn).tag("net").color(true); on one line, mutating the same logger and reading like a sentence, with no separate finished object produced. Which technique enables this?
optionB: Have each setter return a reference to the same logger so calls chain
optionC: Return a fresh copy of the logger from each setter
optionD: Provide one interface that creates a family of loggers
correctAnswerText: Have each setter return a reference to the same logger so calls chain
rationale: Each setter returning a reference to the same object enables the fluent chain on one logger; the distractors prevent chaining or solve unrelated problems.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: Examine:  struct Style { Style& bold(bool b){ b_=b; return *this; } Style& size(int s){ s_=s; return *this; } };  with no terminal method returning a different type. Which idiom does this structure indicate?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Abstract Factory
correctAnswerText: Method Chaining (a fluent interface)
rationale: Mutating setters that return *this with no terminal step producing a distinct product form a fluent interface (Method Chaining). The absence of a build()-style terminal distinguishes it from Builder.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A "fluent" class declares its setters to return a brand-new copy of the object instead of *this. Does this implement method chaining correctly, and what is the effect?
optionA: No — each call mutates a different copy, so accumulated settings can be lost and the chain no longer configures one object
optionB: Yes — returning copies is required for chaining
optionC: Yes — it behaves identically to returning *this
optionD: No — chaining is impossible in C++ regardless
correctAnswerText: No — each call mutates a different copy, so accumulated settings can be lost and the chain no longer configures one object
rationale: Returning a fresh copy each call means each setter acts on a different object, so earlier settings may be discarded; correct chaining returns *this so the same object is configured.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What is the intent of the Prototype pattern?
optionA: Create new objects by copying an existing instance (a prototype)
optionB: Guarantee a single instance
optionC: Let subclasses choose the concrete product
optionD: Assemble an object through ordered steps
correctAnswerText: Create new objects by copying an existing instance (a prototype)
rationale: Prototype creates new objects by cloning an existing prototypical instance. The distractors are Singleton, Factory Method, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Why is Prototype useful when object creation is expensive or configuration-heavy?
optionA: It avoids creating objects at all
optionB: It copies a ready, pre-configured instance instead of rebuilding from scratch
optionC: It guarantees thread safety
optionD: It removes the need for constructors
correctAnswerText: It copies a ready, pre-configured instance instead of rebuilding from scratch
rationale: Cloning a pre-configured prototype reuses its setup, avoiding costly re-construction. The other options misstate Prototype’s benefit.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An editor must duplicate a fully-configured shape (with styling) at runtime without knowing its concrete type. Which pattern fits?
optionB: Abstract Factory
optionC: Method Chaining
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: Duplicating a configured object polymorphically (without its concrete type) is Prototype via clone(); the distractors solve other problems.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct Shape { virtual Shape* clone() const = 0; };  struct Circle : Shape { Shape* clone() const override { return new Circle(*this); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Factory Method
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: A virtual clone() that copy-constructs the same concrete type is the Prototype structure; it copies rather than choosing or assembling a product.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A clone() returns new Base(*this) for every subclass (no override), so cloning a Derived yields a Base. Is this a correct Prototype, and why?
optionB: Yes — provided Base is abstract
optionC: No — Prototype forbids inheritance
optionD: No — without a polymorphic override clone() slices the object, losing the derived type
correctAnswerText: No — without a polymorphic override clone() slices the object, losing the derived type
rationale: Cloning must be polymorphic so each subclass copies its own type; a fixed new Base(*this) slices derived objects, breaking Prototype.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: The Prototype pattern creates new objects by ___.
optionB: sharing a single global instance
optionC: cloning an existing pre-built instance
correctAnswerText: cloning an existing pre-built instance
rationale: Prototype clones an existing instance to make new ones. The distractors are Builder, Singleton, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: What advantage does cloning a prototype give over constructing from scratch?
optionB: It reuses an already-configured instance, avoiding repeated setup
optionC: It guarantees a single instance
correctAnswerText: It reuses an already-configured instance, avoiding repeated setup
rationale: Cloning carries over the prototype’s configuration, saving repeated setup work; the other options are not Prototype benefits.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A game spawns many enemies identical to a configured template at runtime, varying only position. Which pattern most directly applies?
optionB: Singleton
optionC: Factory Method
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: Spawning copies of a configured template is Prototype (clone then tweak); the distractors do not copy a configured instance.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  Document* dup = original->clone();  where clone() is virtual and each subclass returns a copy of itself. Which pattern is this evidence of?
optionB: Abstract Factory
optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: A polymorphic clone() that returns a copy of the concrete subclass is the Prototype pattern; no stepwise build or family creation is involved.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A Prototype holds a raw pointer member and clone() does a shallow copy. What is the risk, and is it a sound clone?
optionA: No risk — shallow copy is always correct
optionB: Sound only if the class is final
optionC: Unsound — Prototype cannot hold pointers
optionD: Risky/unsound — both objects share the same pointed-to resource, so a deep copy is usually needed for an independent clone
correctAnswerText: Risky/unsound — both objects share the same pointed-to resource, so a deep copy is usually needed for an independent clone
rationale: A shallow copy makes clones share the same owned resource, causing aliasing/double-free bugs; an independent clone usually needs a deep copy.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What is the core idea of the Prototype pattern?
optionA: Build the object up one part at a time
optionB: Keep exactly one shared instance
optionC: Create a new object by copying an existing one instead of building it from nothing
optionD: Produce a coordinated family of related objects
correctAnswerText: Create a new object by copying an existing one instead of building it from nothing
rationale: Prototype makes new objects by copying an existing instance rather than constructing from scratch. The distractors describe Builder, Singleton, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Why does cloning rely on a virtual clone() rather than letting the caller write new ConcreteType(original)?
optionA: So the caller can copy an object through a base pointer without knowing or naming its concrete type
optionB: Because virtual methods are faster than constructors
optionC: Because Prototype objects may not have constructors
optionD: Because clone() guarantees the object becomes a Singleton
correctAnswerText: So the caller can copy an object through a base pointer without knowing or naming its concrete type
rationale: A polymorphic clone() lets callers copy through the base interface without knowing the concrete type, which is the whole point of Prototype; naming the concrete type would defeat it.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A diagramming tool lets users press Ctrl+D to duplicate any selected element — a styled node, a connector, a group — at runtime, without the tool knowing each element’s concrete class. Which approach fits?
optionB: Abstract Factory
optionC: Method Chaining
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: Duplicating a configured object polymorphically without naming its concrete type is Prototype via clone(); the distractors do not copy a configured instance.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  struct Node { virtual std::unique_ptr<Node> clone() const = 0; };  struct TextNode : Node { std::unique_ptr<Node> clone() const override { return std::make_unique<TextNode>(*this); } };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Prototype
rationale: A virtual clone() that each concrete type implements to copy-construct itself is the Prototype structure; it copies an existing object rather than choosing or assembling one.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A Prototype subclass owns a pointer to a heap buffer, and its clone() performs only a member-wise (shallow) copy. Is this a sound clone, and why?
optionA: Sound — shallow copies are always correct for Prototype
optionB: Sound only if the class is marked final
optionC: Unsound — both objects end up sharing the same buffer, so a deep copy is needed for a truly independent clone
optionD: Unsound — Prototype classes may not own pointers
correctAnswerText: Unsound — both objects end up sharing the same buffer, so a deep copy is needed for a truly independent clone
rationale: A shallow copy makes the clone and original share the same owned buffer, risking aliasing and double-free; an independent clone usually requires a deep copy of owned resources.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What does the Singleton pattern guarantee?
optionA: A class has exactly one instance with a single global access point
optionB: A class can be freely subclassed and copied
optionC: Objects are produced in matching families
optionD: Objects are built through ordered steps
correctAnswerText: A class has exactly one instance with a single global access point
rationale: Singleton ensures a class has exactly one instance and provides one global access point to it. The other options describe subclassing, Abstract Factory, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: How is the single-instance guarantee typically enforced in C++?
optionB: By making the class abstract
optionC: By a non-public constructor plus a static accessor that returns the sole instance
correctAnswerText: By a non-public constructor plus a static accessor that returns the sole instance
rationale: A hidden (non-public) constructor prevents outside construction, and a static accessor returns the one instance. The other options do not constrain instance count.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: A system needs exactly one shared configuration object, created once and reachable everywhere. Which pattern fits?
optionB: Factory Method
optionC: Abstract Factory
correctAnswerText: Singleton
rationale: One shared instance with a global access point is the Singleton use case; the distractors address copying, deferred subclass choice, and product families.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Cfg { Cfg() = default; public: static Cfg& instance() { static Cfg c; return c; } };  Which pattern does this indicate?
optionB: Factory Method
optionC: Builder
correctAnswerText: Singleton
rationale: A non-public constructor plus a static accessor returning one function-local static is the (Meyers) Singleton idiom; nothing here creates families, products, or copies.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A class exposes a public static getInstance() but also leaves its constructor public. Does this correctly implement Singleton, and why?
optionA: Yes — the static getter is sufficient
optionB: Yes — provided the class is marked final
optionC: No — Singletons must allocate on the heap
optionD: No — a public constructor lets callers create extra instances, breaking the single-instance guarantee
correctAnswerText: No — a public constructor lets callers create extra instances, breaking the single-instance guarantee
rationale: The single-instance invariant requires preventing outside construction; a public constructor allows additional instances, so the implementation is incorrect regardless of the getter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: The Singleton pattern primarily ensures that ___.
optionA: objects are cloned from a prototype
optionB: related product families are produced together
optionC: one instance is reachable from a single global access point
optionD: an object is assembled step by step
correctAnswerText: one instance is reachable from a single global access point
rationale: Singleton ensures a single instance with one global access point. The distractors describe Prototype, Abstract Factory, and Builder.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: Which member combination is characteristic of a C++ Singleton?
optionA: A public constructor and a virtual destructor
optionB: Several overloaded public constructors
optionC: A public clone() method
optionD: A hidden constructor and a static method returning the sole instance
correctAnswerText: A hidden constructor and a static method returning the sole instance
rationale: A hidden constructor blocks external construction while a static accessor returns the one instance. Public/overloaded constructors permit many instances; clone() is Prototype.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: An application must route all logging through one logger object, created on first use and shared app-wide. Which pattern best fits?
optionB: Singleton
