optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Singleton
rationale: A single shared, lazily-created object with global access is Singleton; the distractors solve construction, copying, and interface conversion.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt:
Examine:  // ctor is private
  Logger& logger() { static Logger inst; return inst; }  Which pattern is this idiom?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: Abstract Factory
correctAnswerText: Singleton
rationale: A private constructor plus a function-local static returned by reference is the lazy (Meyers) Singleton; it is not copying, family creation, or fluent chaining.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A developer keeps the instance in a global pointer and calls new in several places “just in case.” Is this a sound Singleton?
optionA: Yes — a global pointer guarantees uniqueness
optionB: Yes — provided it is wrapped in a namespace
optionC: No — multiple new calls create multiple instances, violating the single-instance invariant
optionD: No — Singletons cannot use pointers at all
correctAnswerText: No — multiple new calls create multiple instances, violating the single-instance invariant
rationale: Calling new in several places creates several instances, breaking uniqueness; the access mechanism (global pointer/namespace) does not enforce a single instance.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: In the kitchen-phone analogy from the module, what does Singleton ultimately provide?
optionA: A way to assemble one object part by part
optionB: A way to copy a configured object cheaply
optionC: Exactly one shared object reached through a single access point
optionD: A family of matching objects created together
correctAnswerText: Exactly one shared object reached through a single access point
rationale: Like the one kitchen phone everyone shares, Singleton yields exactly one instance reached through a single access point. The distractors describe Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Why does hiding the constructor matter for a Singleton, and how is it different from simply storing an object in a global variable?
optionA: Because hiding the constructor stops outside code from constructing extra instances, whereas a plain global still lets anyone call the constructor again
optionB: Because a global variable cannot be accessed from other files
optionC: Because Singletons are forbidden from using static storage
optionD: Because hiding the constructor makes the object copyable by default
correctAnswerText: Because hiding the constructor stops outside code from constructing extra instances, whereas a plain global still lets anyone call the constructor again
rationale: A non-public constructor is what enforces uniqueness; a plain global object does not prevent additional construction, so the single-instance invariant is not guaranteed.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++; CodiNeo Creational module content

prompt: A game engine needs one audio mixer: created the first time sound plays, then reused by every system that emits a sound, with no second mixer ever existing. Which approach matches?
optionB: Builder
optionC: Abstract Factory
correctAnswerText: Singleton
rationale: One lazily-created object shared everywhere with no duplicates is the Singleton use case; the distractors solve copying, stepwise construction, and family creation.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Creational module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Clock { Clock(){} public: static Clock& get(){ static Clock c; return c; } long now(); };  Which pattern does this structure indicate?
optionB: Singleton (Meyers function-local static)
optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Singleton (Meyers function-local static)
rationale: A non-public constructor plus a static accessor returning a single function-local static is the Meyers Singleton idiom; nothing here copies, chains, or chooses a product.
sourceReferences: Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A class offers a static current() accessor but its copy constructor is still public, so callers can write Settings s2 = Settings::current();. Does this faithfully implement Singleton?
optionA: Yes — a static accessor is all that is required
optionB: Yes — copying is encouraged so each caller gets its own settings
optionC: No — a public copy constructor lets callers duplicate the instance, breaking the single-instance guarantee
optionD: No — Singletons must be allocated with new
correctAnswerText: No — a public copy constructor lets callers duplicate the instance, breaking the single-instance guarantee
rationale: Uniqueness must be protected against copying too; a public copy constructor lets callers clone the instance, violating the invariant regardless of the accessor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What is the intent of the Adapter pattern?
optionA: Convert a class’s interface into another interface clients expect, so incompatible types can work together
optionB: Add new behaviour to an object by wrapping it
optionC: Control or restrict access to an object
optionD: Ensure a class has only one instance
correctAnswerText: Convert a class’s interface into another interface clients expect, so incompatible types can work together
rationale: Adapter converts one existing interface into the interface a client expects. The distractors describe Decorator, Proxy, and Singleton.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: How does an Adapter make two incompatible interfaces work together?
optionA: It edits the adaptee’s source code to match the client
optionB: It implements the target interface and translates each call to the adaptee’s existing methods
optionC: It duplicates the adaptee for each client
optionD: It deletes the target interface entirely
correctAnswerText: It implements the target interface and translates each call to the adaptee’s existing methods
rationale: The adapter conforms to the target interface and forwards/translates calls to the unchanged adaptee. It does not modify or copy the adaptee.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A new reporting client expects a JSON-exporter interface, but the only library available exposes a completely different XML API you cannot change. Which pattern lets them work together?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Adapter
rationale: Bridging a required interface to an unchangeable, differently-shaped existing one is Adapter. The distractors add behaviour, control access, or build trees.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class JsonTarget { public: virtual std::string toJson() = 0; };  class XmlLib { public: std::string toXml(); };  class XmlToJson : public JsonTarget { XmlLib lib; public: std::string toJson() override { return convert(lib.toXml()); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Facade
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Adapter
rationale: A class implementing the expected interface (JsonTarget) by translating to an existing, differently-shaped API (XmlLib) is Adapter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class claims to be an Adapter but simply adds caching around the very same interface the client already used, converting nothing. Is this correctly an Adapter?
optionA: No — Adapter must convert one interface into another; adding behaviour over the same interface is Decorator/Proxy, not Adapter
optionB: Yes — any wrapper is an Adapter
optionC: Yes — caching counts as adapting
correctAnswerText: No — Adapter must convert one interface into another; adding behaviour over the same interface is Decorator/Proxy, not Adapter
rationale: Adapter’s defining purpose is interface conversion. Adding behaviour while keeping the same interface is Decorator/Proxy intent, not Adapter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: The Adapter pattern is best described as a way to ___.
optionB: make one existing interface usable through a different, expected interface
optionC: share intrinsic state across many objects
optionD: pass a request along a chain of handlers
correctAnswerText: make one existing interface usable through a different, expected interface
rationale: Adapter makes an existing interface usable through the interface a client expects. The distractors are Singleton, Flyweight, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What is the adapter’s role relative to the adaptee and the client?
optionA: It changes the client to match the adaptee
optionB: It merges client and adaptee into one class
optionC: It conforms to the interface the client expects and delegates work to the adaptee
optionD: It hides the adaptee permanently from all code
correctAnswerText: It conforms to the interface the client expects and delegates work to the adaptee
rationale: The adapter presents the client-expected interface and delegates to the adaptee, leaving both client and adaptee unchanged.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An app’s payment module calls charge(Money), but a third-party SDK you cannot modify only offers processPayment(double, std::string). Which pattern bridges them?
optionB: Facade
optionC: Decorator
correctAnswerText: Adapter
rationale: Reconciling a required call shape with an unchangeable foreign API is Adapter. The distractors build trees, simplify subsystems, or add behaviour.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Logger { public: virtual void log(std::string) = 0; };  class LegacyWriter { public: void writeLine(const char*); };  class LegacyLogAdapter : public Logger { LegacyWriter w; public: void log(std::string s) override { w.writeLine(s.c_str()); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Decorator
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Adapter
rationale: Implementing the expected Logger interface by translating to a legacy method with a different signature is Adapter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A wrapper implements the expected RateProvider interface and internally calls an unrelated CurrencyApi with a different signature, translating the arguments. Is calling this an Adapter defensible?
optionB: Yes — it implements the expected interface and converts calls to a different existing interface, which is exactly Adapter
optionC: No — this is always Facade
optionD: Yes — but only if it also adds new features
correctAnswerText: Yes — it implements the expected interface and converts calls to a different existing interface, which is exactly Adapter
rationale: Implementing the expected interface while converting calls to a differently-shaped existing API is the definition of Adapter; argument translation is normal.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An Adapter primarily exists to ___.
optionA: let one existing interface be used through the different interface a caller expects
optionB: attach optional behaviour to an object at runtime
optionC: give a caller a controlled stand-in for a costly object
optionD: merge many small objects into a shared pool
correctAnswerText: let one existing interface be used through the different interface a caller expects
rationale: Adapter performs interface conversion so an existing component is usable through the expected interface. The distractors describe Decorator, Proxy, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: When an adapter receives a call, what does it actually do with the wrapped (adaptee) object?
optionA: It rewrites the adaptee so the two interfaces become identical
optionB: It forwards the call to the adaptee, translating names and argument types as needed
optionC: It stores the adaptee and discards every incoming call
optionD: It copies the adaptee once per caller to avoid sharing
correctAnswerText: It forwards the call to the adaptee, translating names and argument types as needed
rationale: The adapter conforms to the expected interface and delegates each call to the unchanged adaptee, translating signatures. It never edits or duplicates the adaptee.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Your analytics layer is written against a Geocoder type with lookup(Address), but the only mapping vendor you can use ships a frozen SDK whose method is resolve(const char*, double&, double&). You may not edit the SDK. What design lets your layer call it unchanged?
optionA: A subsystem front that hides the SDK behind one easy method
optionB: A wrapper that adds caching to the SDK while keeping its interface
optionC: A tree of vendor objects treated uniformly
optionD: A class implementing Geocoder that translates each lookup into the SDK’s resolve call
correctAnswerText: A class implementing Geocoder that translates each lookup into the SDK’s resolve call
rationale: A class that presents the expected Geocoder interface while converting calls to the differently-shaped, unmodifiable SDK is an Adapter. The distractors are Facade, Decorator, and Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Bridge, because the name says so and it holds another object
optionB: Decorator, because it changes the returned value
optionC: Adapter, because it implements the expected interface by converting calls to a differently-shaped existing API
optionD: Proxy, because it stands in for the probe
correctAnswerText: Adapter, because it implements the expected interface by converting calls to a differently-shaped existing API
rationale: Despite its name, ProbeBridge implements the expected TempSensor interface and converts a Kelvin reading into the expected Celsius call — interface conversion, i.e. Adapter. It does not vary two hierarchies (Bridge), add client-facing features (Decorator), or control access (Proxy).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class advertised as an Adapter implements the very same interface its caller already used and only records timing metrics before forwarding each call unchanged — no signatures are converted. Is the Adapter label correct?
optionA: Yes — any class that wraps another is an Adapter
optionB: Yes — forwarding calls is the essence of Adapter
optionC: No — because Adapters are forbidden from holding the wrapped object
optionD: No — with no interface conversion this adds behaviour over the same interface, which is Decorator/Proxy intent, not Adapter
correctAnswerText: No — with no interface conversion this adds behaviour over the same interface, which is Decorator/Proxy intent, not Adapter
rationale: Adapter is defined by converting one interface into another. Keeping the same interface while adding work (metrics) is Decorator or Proxy intent; calling it Adapter misstates the intent.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Bridge pattern?
optionB: Provide a surrogate for access
optionC: Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two can vary independently
correctAnswerText: Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two can vary independently
rationale: Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation so both vary independently. The distractors are Decorator, Proxy, and Composite.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: How does Bridge differ from plain inheritance for handling two dimensions of variation?
optionA: It uses one giant subclass per combination
optionB: It forbids polymorphism
optionC: It merges both dimensions into one class
optionD: It composes the abstraction with a separate implementor hierarchy, avoiding a combinatorial explosion of subclasses
correctAnswerText: It composes the abstraction with a separate implementor hierarchy, avoiding a combinatorial explosion of subclasses
rationale: Bridge composes (not inherits) abstraction with an independent implementor hierarchy, avoiding the N×M subclass explosion.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Shapes (circle, square) must render via multiple drawing backends (OpenGL, SVG), and both sets will keep growing independently. Which pattern avoids one subclass per shape×backend pair?
optionB: Decorator
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Bridge
rationale: Two independently-varying dimensions handled by composition is Bridge. The distractors add behaviour, convert, or build trees.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Renderer { public: virtual void drawCircle(float) = 0; };  class Shape { protected: Renderer* r; public: Shape(Renderer* rr) : r(rr) {} virtual void draw() = 0; };  class Circle : public Shape { float rad; public: void draw() override { r->drawCircle(rad); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Bridge
rationale: An abstraction hierarchy (Shape) holding and delegating to a separate implementor hierarchy (Renderer) that both vary independently is Bridge.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A developer claims a class uses Bridge because it holds a pointer to an implementation object. But there is only one implementation, used solely to hide private members from the header. Is this Bridge?
optionB: Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are the same
optionC: No — that is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
correctAnswerText: No — that is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
rationale: A single fixed implementation pointer used only to hide members is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge needs genuine, independently varying abstraction and implementor hierarchies.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Bridge’s intent is to ___.
optionB: encapsulate requests as objects
optionC: traverse a collection
optionD: separate an abstraction from its implementation so each varies independently
correctAnswerText: separate an abstraction from its implementation so each varies independently
rationale: Bridge separates abstraction from implementation so each side changes independently. The distractors are Flyweight, Command, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What distinguishes Bridge from the PIMPL idiom?
optionA: Bridge supports multiple interchangeable implementations of an abstraction, while PIMPL hides one implementation to stabilise a header
optionB: They are identical
optionC: PIMPL allows many implementations; Bridge allows only one
correctAnswerText: Bridge supports multiple interchangeable implementations of an abstraction, while PIMPL hides one implementation to stabilise a header
rationale: Bridge’s implementor hierarchy genuinely varies; PIMPL has a single hidden implementation whose purpose is header insulation.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: A messaging abstraction (alert, reminder) must work over several delivery channels (email, SMS, push), where both the message types and the channels expand over time, independently. Which pattern?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: Bridge
rationale: Two independently-growing dimensions (message kinds × channels) handled by composition is Bridge.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Device { public: virtual void on() = 0; };  class Remote { protected: Device* dev; public: Remote(Device* d) : dev(d) {} virtual void power() { dev->on(); } };  class TV : public Device { void on() override; };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Bridge
correctAnswerText: Bridge
rationale: An abstraction (Remote) composed with an independent implementor hierarchy (Device) that both vary is Bridge.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A design has an abstract Shape hierarchy, each holding a pointer to an abstract Renderer hierarchy, and new shapes and new renderers are added without touching each other. Is Bridge a correct description?
optionB: No — Bridge needs a single implementation
optionC: Yes — but only if there is one renderer
optionD: Yes — an abstraction hierarchy composed with an independently varying implementor hierarchy is exactly Bridge
correctAnswerText: Yes — an abstraction hierarchy composed with an independently varying implementor hierarchy is exactly Bridge
rationale: Two varying hierarchies joined by composition, each extensible without the other, is the defining Bridge structure.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: The intent of Bridge is to ___.
optionA: add responsibilities to an object by wrapping it
optionB: decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two hierarchies can vary independently
optionC: provide a simplified interface to a subsystem
optionD: share intrinsic state across many objects
correctAnswerText: decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two hierarchies can vary independently
rationale: Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation so each side evolves on its own. The distractors are Decorator, Facade, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Why does Bridge use composition between an abstraction and an implementor hierarchy instead of one big inheritance tree?
optionA: Because composing the two hierarchies lets each grow separately and avoids an N×M explosion of combined subclasses
optionB: Because composition makes the abstraction a Singleton
optionC: Because inheritance cannot use virtual functions
optionD: Because it merges both dimensions into a single class
correctAnswerText: Because composing the two hierarchies lets each grow separately and avoids an N×M explosion of combined subclasses
rationale: Bridge composes an abstraction with an independent implementor hierarchy, so adding a new abstraction or a new implementation does not require a subclass for every pairing — avoiding the combinatorial explosion inheritance would cause.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A reporting product has report kinds (summary, audit, forecast) that must each be emitted through several output engines (PDF, HTML, spreadsheet), and both the set of report kinds and the set of engines are expected to keep growing on their own schedules. Which design avoids one class per report-kind×engine pair?
optionA: A surrogate that defers building each report
optionB: An abstraction hierarchy of report kinds that holds and delegates to a separate, independently extensible engine hierarchy
optionC: A class converting one engine’s interface into another’s
optionD: A pool that shares one report instance per kind
correctAnswerText: An abstraction hierarchy of report kinds that holds and delegates to a separate, independently extensible engine hierarchy
rationale: Two dimensions that vary independently, joined by composition so each grows without the other, is Bridge. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: PIMPL, because Alert holds a pointer to another type
optionB: Decorator, because Alert wraps a Channel
optionC: Composite, because alerts can be nested
optionD: Bridge, because an abstraction hierarchy (Alert) is composed with a separate, independently varying implementor hierarchy (Channel)
correctAnswerText: Bridge, because an abstraction hierarchy (Alert) is composed with a separate, independently varying implementor hierarchy (Channel)
rationale: Alert/CriticalAlert form an abstraction hierarchy that holds and delegates to an independent Channel implementor hierarchy; both vary independently — Bridge. It is not PIMPL (which hides one fixed implementation to stabilise a header, with no varying abstraction), not Decorator, and not Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class holds a single std::unique_ptr<Impl> whose only role is to keep its private members out of the public header; there is exactly one Impl and no abstraction varies. A teammate labels this “Bridge.” Is that accurate?
optionA: Yes — any pointer to an implementation object is Bridge
optionB: Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are two names for the same construct
optionC: No — one fixed implementation used solely for header insulation is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
optionD: No — because Bridge cannot legally use a smart pointer
correctAnswerText: No — one fixed implementation used solely for header insulation is the PIMPL idiom; Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
rationale: A single, fixed implementation pointer used only to hide members is PIMPL, a C++ idiom. Bridge requires a genuinely varying abstraction hierarchy and an independently varying implementor hierarchy — absent here.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Composite pattern?
optionB: Add behaviour by wrapping
optionC: Compose objects into part-whole trees and let clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
optionD: Provide a surrogate for access control
correctAnswerText: Compose objects into part-whole trees and let clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
rationale: Composite builds part-whole trees treated uniformly. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What makes uniform treatment of leaves and composites possible?
optionB: Composites store no children
optionC: A global registry maps node types
optionD: Both leaf and composite implement the same component interface
correctAnswerText: Both leaf and composite implement the same component interface
rationale: A shared component interface lets clients call the same operations on a leaf or a whole subtree without distinguishing them.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A drawing app must treat a single shape and a group of shapes identically for operations like move() and draw(), with groups nesting arbitrarily. Which pattern?
optionB: Decorator
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Composite
rationale: Uniform treatment of single items and nested groups is Composite. The distractors add behaviour, control access, or swap algorithms.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Node { public: virtual int size() = 0; };  class File : public Node { int size() override { return sz; } };  class Folder : public Node { std::vector<Node*> kids; public: int size() override { int t = 0; for (auto* k : kids) t += k->size(); return t; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Decorator
correctAnswerText: Composite
rationale: A shared Node interface where a container recursively aggregates its children is the Composite part-whole structure.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A “Composite” design gives Folder a children list but File a totally different interface with unrelated method names, forcing clients to type-check each node. Does this satisfy Composite’s intent?
optionA: Yes — as long as there is a tree
optionB: Yes — composites and leaves should differ
optionC: No — Composite requires a shared component interface so leaf and composite are treated uniformly; type-checking defeats it
correctAnswerText: No — Composite requires a shared component interface so leaf and composite are treated uniformly; type-checking defeats it
rationale: Uniform treatment via a common interface is the whole point; if clients must branch on node type, the pattern’s benefit is lost.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Composite is used to ___.
optionB: encapsulate a request as an object
optionC: define interchangeable algorithms
optionD: represent part-whole hierarchies so individual and grouped objects are used the same way
correctAnswerText: represent part-whole hierarchies so individual and grouped objects are used the same way
rationale: Composite represents part-whole hierarchies treated uniformly. The distractors are Flyweight, Command, and Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: In Composite, how does a composite node usually implement an operation?
optionA: It delegates the operation to each of its children and combines the results
optionB: It ignores its children
optionC: It converts the child interface
correctAnswerText: It delegates the operation to each of its children and combines the results
rationale: A composite forwards the operation to each child and aggregates, which is what makes recursive trees work uniformly.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An org chart must compute total headcount whether you ask an individual employee or a whole department containing sub-departments, using one call. Which pattern?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Composite
rationale: Recursive part-whole aggregation through a common interface is Composite.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Component { public: virtual double price() = 0; };  class Item : public Component { double price() override { return p; } };  class Box : public Component { std::vector<Component*> items; public: double price() override { double t = 0; for (auto* i : items) t += i->price(); return t; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: Composite
correctAnswerText: Composite
rationale: A shared Component interface with a container recursively summing children’s prices is Composite.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A team models a menu tree where both MenuItem and Menu implement the same MenuComponent interface, and Menu forwards print() to its children. Is this a sound Composite?
optionA: No — Composite needs different interfaces per node
optionB: No — menus cannot nest
optionC: Yes — but only with exactly two levels
optionD: Yes — a shared interface plus recursive delegation to children is exactly Composite’s part-whole structure
correctAnswerText: Yes — a shared interface plus recursive delegation to children is exactly Composite’s part-whole structure
rationale: A common interface across leaf and container plus recursive delegation is the correct Composite structure.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: The Composite pattern is used to ___.
optionA: control access to an expensive object through a surrogate
optionB: share fine-grained objects to save memory
optionC: convert an existing interface into another
optionD: compose objects into part-whole trees so individual objects and whole groups are treated uniformly
correctAnswerText: compose objects into part-whole trees so individual objects and whole groups are treated uniformly
rationale: Composite builds part-whole trees in which leaves and containers are used the same way. The distractors are Proxy, Flyweight, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What makes it possible for a client to call the same operation on a single element and on a whole nested group without checking which it has?
optionA: The leaf type secretly inherits from the container type
optionB: Both the leaf and the container implement one shared component interface
optionC: A global registry records every node’s concrete type
optionD: Containers are forbidden from holding other containers
correctAnswerText: Both the leaf and the container implement one shared component interface
rationale: A common component interface implemented by both leaf and container is what enables uniform treatment; the client never branches on node type. Recursion across sub-containers is exactly what Composite supports.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A budgeting tool must answer total() for either a single line item or an envelope that contains line items and other envelopes nested to any depth, using one identical call regardless of which it is handed. Which design fits?
optionA: A shared component interface where containers recursively forward total() to their children
optionB: A wrapper that adds new behaviour to each line item
optionC: A surrogate that controls access to each envelope
optionD: A class converting envelopes into a reporting interface
correctAnswerText: A shared component interface where containers recursively forward total() to their children
rationale: Uniform treatment of single items and arbitrarily nested groups via recursive aggregation through a shared interface is Composite. The distractors are Decorator, Proxy, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionB: Bridge, because two hierarchies are involved
optionC: Composite, because leaf and container share one interface and the container recursively aggregates its children
correctAnswerText: Composite, because leaf and container share one interface and the container recursively aggregates its children
rationale: Chore (leaf) and Project (container) both implement Task, and Project recursively sums its children — the part-whole structure of Composite. There is no added responsibility (Decorator), no second varying hierarchy (Bridge), and no shared-state pooling (Flyweight).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A so-called Composite gives the container a children list and a render() method, but the leaf type exposes a wholly different method named paint() with no shared interface, so clients must test each node’s type before calling. Does this honour Composite’s intent?
optionA: Yes — leaves and containers are supposed to differ
optionB: Yes — having a tree is all Composite requires
optionC: No — Composite requires a shared component interface so leaf and container are used uniformly; forcing clients to type-check defeats it
optionD: No — Composite forbids containers from storing children
correctAnswerText: No — Composite requires a shared component interface so leaf and container are used uniformly; forcing clients to type-check defeats it
rationale: Uniform treatment through a common interface is the whole point of Composite. If clients must branch on node type, the pattern’s benefit is lost. Containers storing children is required, not forbidden.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Decorator pattern?
optionA: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
optionB: Convert one interface into another
optionC: Ensure a class has one instance
correctAnswerText: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
rationale: Decorator adds responsibilities to an object dynamically through wrapping. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Why can decorators be stacked on top of one another?
optionA: Because they edit the base class
optionB: Each decorator implements the same component interface and holds a component, so they nest
optionC: Because they share one global instance
optionD: Because they convert interfaces between layers
correctAnswerText: Each decorator implements the same component interface and holds a component, so they nest
rationale: Each decorator is itself a component and wraps a component, so wrappers nest indefinitely while staying interchangeable with the base.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: You must add optional, combinable features (encryption, compression, buffering) to a data stream at runtime without a subclass explosion, keeping the same stream interface. Which pattern?
optionB: Adapter
optionC: Decorator
correctAnswerText: Decorator
rationale: Dynamically composable responsibilities over a shared interface is Decorator. The distractors control access, convert, or simplify subsystems.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Coffee { public: virtual int cost() = 0; };  class Basic : public Coffee { int cost() override { return 2; } };  class MilkDecorator : public Coffee { Coffee* inner; public: MilkDecorator(Coffee* c) : inner(c) {} int cost() override { return inner->cost() + 1; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Adapter
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Decorator
rationale: A same-interface wrapper that augments the wrapped object’s result (adds cost) is Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A wrapper implements the same interface and adds a visible new feature (drawing a border) to what the client sees, delegating the rest. Best classified as?
optionA: Decorator — it adds new client-facing behaviour while preserving the interface
optionB: Proxy — any wrapper controls access
optionC: Adapter — it converts the interface
optionD: Bridge — it splits abstraction from implementation
correctAnswerText: Decorator — it adds new client-facing behaviour while preserving the interface
rationale: Adding new client-facing responsibility while keeping the component interface is Decorator, distinct from Proxy’s access-control intent.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Decorator lets you ___.
optionB: add responsibilities to individual objects dynamically without affecting other objects
optionC: convert one interface to another
correctAnswerText: add responsibilities to individual objects dynamically without affecting other objects
rationale: Decorator adds responsibilities to individual objects at runtime. The distractors are Singleton, Adapter, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What must a decorator share with the object it wraps?
optionB: A single global instance
optionC: The same abstract interface, so it is interchangeable with the wrapped object
optionD: A different interface to force conversion
correctAnswerText: The same abstract interface, so it is interchangeable with the wrapped object
rationale: Sharing the component interface is what lets a decorated object be used anywhere the undecorated one is, and lets wrappers nest.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A text view should optionally gain scrollbars and borders in any combination chosen at runtime, all usable wherever a plain view is expected. Which pattern?
optionB: Adapter
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Decorator
rationale: Runtime-composable, interchangeable add-ons over a shared interface is Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Notifier { public: virtual void send(std::string) = 0; };  class Email : public Notifier { void send(std::string) override; };  class SmsDecorator : public Notifier { Notifier* wrap; public: SmsDecorator(Notifier* n) : wrap(n) {} void send(std::string m) override { wrap->send(m); sendSms(m); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Composite
correctAnswerText: Decorator
rationale: A same-interface wrapper that performs the wrapped action and adds extra behaviour (an SMS) is Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A wrapper with the same interface only adds lazy initialization and access checks, not new client-facing features. Is “Decorator” the right classification?
optionA: Yes — every wrapper is a Decorator
optionB: No — controlling access without adding new client-facing behaviour is Proxy, not Decorator
optionC: Yes — lazy init is a new feature, so Decorator
optionD: No — it must be an Adapter
correctAnswerText: No — controlling access without adding new client-facing behaviour is Proxy, not Decorator
rationale: Access control/lazy loading without new responsibilities is Proxy intent; Decorator specifically adds new client-facing behaviour.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: According to this module, the Decorator pattern primarily lets a program ___.
optionA: guarantee a single shared instance of a class
optionB: attach extra responsibilities to an individual object at runtime by wrapping it
optionC: simplify a complex subsystem behind one front-door class
optionD: represent a part-whole hierarchy as a tree
correctAnswerText: attach extra responsibilities to an individual object at runtime by wrapping it
rationale: Decorator dynamically adds responsibilities to an object through wrapping. The distractors are Singleton, Facade, and Composite.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What property of a decorator allows several of them to be layered on the same object?
optionA: Each decorator stores a single global instance the others reuse
optionB: Each decorator changes the wrapped object’s interface as it nests
optionC: Each decorator edits the base class so later layers can attach
optionD: Each decorator both implements the component interface and holds a component, so the wrapper is itself decoratable
correctAnswerText: Each decorator both implements the component interface and holds a component, so the wrapper is itself decoratable
rationale: Because a decorator is itself a component that holds a component, decorators nest indefinitely while staying interchangeable with the undecorated object. They neither edit the base class nor change the interface.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A message-sending service must let operators turn on any mix of retry, rate-limiting, and audit-logging per outgoing message at runtime, with each combination still usable wherever a plain sender is expected — and you want to avoid one subclass per combination. Which design fits?
optionA: A surrogate that defers creating the sender until first use
optionB: A class converting the sender into a third-party transport interface
optionC: A set of same-interface wrappers, each adding one behaviour around the forwarded send and stackable in any order
optionD: A simplified front that hides the sending subsystem behind one call
correctAnswerText: A set of same-interface wrappers, each adding one behaviour around the forwarded send and stackable in any order
rationale: Composable, interchangeable add-ons over a shared interface, combined at runtime, is Decorator. The distractors are Proxy, Adapter, and Facade.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Proxy, because it wraps another Report
optionB: Composite, because it holds a Report
optionC: Adapter, because it changes the output
optionD: Decorator, because it shares the component interface and adds a visible new responsibility around the forwarded call, and can itself be wrapped
correctAnswerText: Decorator, because it shares the component interface and adds a visible new responsibility around the forwarded call, and can itself be wrapped
rationale: WatermarkLayer keeps the Report interface, augments the wrapped object’s result with new client-facing content, and is itself a Report so it can stack — Decorator. Proxy would only control access; Adapter would convert the interface; Composite aggregates many children.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A same-interface wrapper around a Connection only adds lazy opening of the socket and a permission check before delegating; it exposes no new operation or output to the caller. Is “Decorator” the most precise label?
optionA: No — lazy opening and access checks without any new client-facing behaviour are Proxy intent, not Decorator
optionB: Yes — every same-interface wrapper is a Decorator
optionC: Yes — lazy opening counts as a new responsibility, so Decorator
optionD: No — this can only be an Adapter
correctAnswerText: No — lazy opening and access checks without any new client-facing behaviour are Proxy intent, not Decorator
rationale: Decorator specifically adds new client-facing responsibilities. Controlling access and deferring creation, with nothing new exposed, is Proxy intent. It is not interface conversion, so not Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Facade pattern?
optionA: Provide a single, simplified interface to a larger body of code or a complex subsystem
optionB: Convert one interface to another
optionC: Ensure a single instance
correctAnswerText: Provide a single, simplified interface to a larger body of code or a complex subsystem
rationale: Facade offers a simplified entry point to a subsystem. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Does a Facade hide or remove the subsystem’s classes?
optionA: It permanently removes the subsystem classes
optionB: It provides a simpler entry point while the subsystem classes remain accessible for advanced use
optionC: It converts each subsystem class’s interface
optionD: It guarantees only one subsystem object exists
correctAnswerText: It provides a simpler entry point while the subsystem classes remain accessible for advanced use
rationale: A Facade adds a convenient interface but does not forbid direct use of the subsystem when needed.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Clients must start a video conversion with one easy call, while behind it many components (codec, audio mixer, muxer) cooperate. Which pattern offers the simple front door?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: Facade
rationale: A single simplified entry point over a multi-part subsystem is Facade. The distractors convert, control access, or split abstraction.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class ComputerStarter { CPU cpu; Memory mem; Disk disk; public: void boot() { cpu.freeze(); mem.load(0, disk.read(0)); cpu.jump(0); cpu.execute(); } };  A single class offering one method that orchestrates several subsystem objects illustrates which pattern?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Facade
rationale: One class wrapping a multi-step subsystem interaction behind a simple method (boot) is a Facade.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class exposes one boot() method that calls several subsystem objects in sequence so callers don’t deal with them directly, but the subsystems don’t communicate back through it. Best classified as?
optionA: Facade — it simplifies one-way access to a subsystem without mediating peer-to-peer communication
optionB: Mediator — any coordinator is a Mediator
optionC: Adapter — it converts interfaces
correctAnswerText: Facade — it simplifies one-way access to a subsystem without mediating peer-to-peer communication
rationale: One-way simplification of subsystem access is Facade; Mediator would coordinate two-way communication among peers.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: A Facade primarily ___.
optionB: offers a simple, unified interface over a complex subsystem
optionC: encapsulates a request as an object
correctAnswerText: offers a simple, unified interface over a complex subsystem
rationale: Facade offers a unified, simple interface over a subsystem. The distractors are Flyweight, Command, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Using a Facade, can clients still bypass it to use subsystem classes directly?
optionA: No — the subsystem becomes private forever
optionB: No — the Facade deletes the subsystem
optionC: Yes — a Facade adds a convenient interface without forbidding direct subsystem access
optionD: Only if the Facade is a Singleton
correctAnswerText: Yes — a Facade adds a convenient interface without forbidding direct subsystem access
rationale: A Facade is additive: it simplifies common use but leaves the subsystem reachable for advanced needs.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A home-theatre app should let users press one “watch movie” button that configures amplifier, lights, projector, and player, hiding the steps. Which pattern?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Facade
rationale: A one-call simplified front over many subsystem steps is Facade.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class OrderProcessor { Inventory inv; Payment pay; Shipping ship; public: void place(Order o) { inv.reserve(o); pay.charge(o); ship.dispatch(o); } };  A single class offering one method that coordinates several subsystems for the client is an example of ___.
optionB: Strategy
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: Facade
rationale: Wrapping a multi-subsystem workflow behind one method (place) is a Facade.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A so-called Facade not only simplifies calls but also forces all subsystem objects to communicate exclusively through it, coordinating their mutual interactions. Is “Facade” still the most precise label?
optionB: No — centralising mutual communication between objects is Mediator’s intent, not Facade’s
optionC: Yes — Facade and Mediator are identical
correctAnswerText: No — centralising mutual communication between objects is Mediator’s intent, not Facade’s
rationale: Once it governs two-way communication among objects, the intent matches Mediator; Facade is strictly one-way simplification.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: A Facade is introduced in order to ___.
optionA: offer a single simplified interface over a larger or more complex subsystem
optionB: convert one existing interface into another
optionC: attach extra responsibilities to an object dynamically
optionD: guarantee a class has exactly one instance
correctAnswerText: offer a single simplified interface over a larger or more complex subsystem
rationale: Facade provides a simplified front-door interface to a subsystem. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Singleton.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: After a Facade is added, what happens to the underlying subsystem classes?
optionA: They remain reachable for advanced needs; the Facade only adds a convenient high-level entry point
optionB: They are deleted, since the Facade replaces them
optionC: Each of their interfaces is converted into the Facade’s interface
optionD: They are reduced to a single shared instance
correctAnswerText: They remain reachable for advanced needs; the Facade only adds a convenient high-level entry point
rationale: A Facade is additive: it simplifies common usage while leaving the subsystem classes accessible when finer control is required. It neither removes them nor converts their interfaces.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A checkout button should trigger one call that internally reserves stock, charges the card, books a courier, and emails a receipt, so callers never touch those four components directly and the components do not call back through it. Which design fits?
optionA: A surrogate that controls access to each of the four components
optionB: A wrapper that adds new behaviour to each component in a stack
optionC: A hub through which the four components route all their mutual messages
optionD: One class that owns the four components and exposes a single high-level method choreographing them one-way
correctAnswerText: One class that owns the four components and exposes a single high-level method choreographing them one-way
rationale: A single class exposing one high-level method that orchestrates several subsystem objects one-way is a Facade. The distractors are Proxy, Decorator, and Mediator (which would coordinate two-way peer communication).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Mediator, because four components are involved
optionB: Adapter, because it converts the clip
optionC: Facade, because one class exposes a single high-level method that choreographs several subsystem objects one-way
optionD: Composite, because the steps form a tree
correctAnswerText: Facade, because one class exposes a single high-level method that choreographs several subsystem objects one-way
rationale: MediaExport wraps a multi-step subsystem interaction behind one simple method (run), calling the components in sequence without them communicating back through it — a Facade. Mediator would govern two-way peer communication; Adapter converts interfaces; Composite builds part-whole trees.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class billed as a Facade not only offers simple entry methods but also forces every subsystem object to route all of its mutual messages through it, coordinating their back-and-forth interactions. Is “Facade” still the most precise label?
optionA: Yes — Facade is responsible for coordinating communication
optionB: No — centralising mutual, two-way communication among peers is Mediator’s intent; Facade is strictly one-way simplification
optionC: Yes — Facade and Mediator describe the same role
optionD: No — this is actually an Adapter
correctAnswerText: No — centralising mutual, two-way communication among peers is Mediator’s intent; Facade is strictly one-way simplification
rationale: Once a class governs two-way communication among a set of peers, its intent matches Mediator. A Facade only simplifies one-way access to a subsystem; it does not mediate peer interactions.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Flyweight pattern?
optionB: Add behaviour by wrapping
optionC: Share common state to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently
correctAnswerText: Share common state to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently
rationale: Flyweight minimises memory by sharing state across many similar objects. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What is the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic state in Flyweight?
optionA: Intrinsic is per-client; extrinsic is shared
optionB: Both are stored in the flyweight
optionC: Both are supplied by the client each call
optionD: Intrinsic state is shared and stored in the flyweight; extrinsic state is context-dependent and supplied by the client
correctAnswerText: Intrinsic state is shared and stored in the flyweight; extrinsic state is context-dependent and supplied by the client
rationale: Intrinsic (shared) state lives in the flyweight; extrinsic (context) state is passed in by the client per operation.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A text editor renders millions of character glyphs; each glyph’s font and shape repeat, but its position differs. How do you cut memory by sharing the repeating data?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Flyweight
rationale: Sharing repeated intrinsic data while supplying per-use position is Flyweight. The distractors build trees, control access, or add behaviour.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Glyph { char c; Font* font; public: void draw(int x, int y); };  Glyph* GlyphFactory::get(char c) { if (!pool.count(c)) pool[c] = new Glyph(c); return pool[c]; }  Position (x, y) is passed to draw(). Which pattern is this?
optionB: Flyweight
optionC: Singleton
correctAnswerText: Flyweight
rationale: A factory pooling shared instances by intrinsic key (char/font) while position is passed in (extrinsic) is Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A “Flyweight” stores each object’s unique screen position as a member and never shares instances, creating one object per character. Does this achieve Flyweight’s goal?
optionA: Yes — Flyweight stores all state internally
optionB: Yes — one object per character is the goal
optionC: No — keeping per-instance extrinsic state and not sharing defeats Flyweight; position should be extrinsic and instances shared
correctAnswerText: No — keeping per-instance extrinsic state and not sharing defeats Flyweight; position should be extrinsic and instances shared
rationale: Flyweight’s memory savings come from sharing instances and externalising context state; storing position per instance and not sharing negates the benefit.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Flyweight is used to ___.
optionA: encapsulate a request as an object
optionB: define interchangeable algorithms
optionC: traverse a collection
optionD: support large numbers of similar objects efficiently by sharing their common state
correctAnswerText: support large numbers of similar objects efficiently by sharing their common state
rationale: Flyweight supports many similar objects by sharing common state. The distractors are Command, Strategy, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Where is extrinsic state kept in a Flyweight design?
optionA: Outside the flyweight — supplied by the client or context when an operation runs
optionB: Permanently inside each shared flyweight
optionC: In a single global Singleton only
correctAnswerText: Outside the flyweight — supplied by the client or context when an operation runs
rationale: Extrinsic state is context-dependent and provided by the client at call time, so it must not be stored in the shared flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A forest scene draws 1,000,000 trees of a few species; mesh and texture repeat per species while location varies per tree. How do you avoid storing the mesh a million times?
optionB: Flyweight
optionC: Bridge
correctAnswerText: Flyweight
rationale: Sharing per-species intrinsic data while passing per-tree position is Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class TreeType { std::string name; Texture tex; public: void render(int x, int y); };  TreeType* TreeFactory::of(std::string n) { auto it = pool.find(n); if (it != pool.end()) return it->second; return pool[n] = new TreeType(n); }  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Builder
optionC: Flyweight
correctAnswerText: Flyweight
rationale: A factory returning shared instances keyed by intrinsic data, with position passed to render(), is Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A renderer keeps one shared ParticleType (color, sprite) per kind and passes each particle’s x/y/velocity at draw time. Is this a correct Flyweight?
optionA: No — Flyweight must store position internally
optionB: No — Flyweight cannot use a factory
optionC: Yes — but only for fewer than 100 objects
optionD: Yes — sharing intrinsic data while passing extrinsic state at call time is exactly Flyweight
correctAnswerText: Yes — sharing intrinsic data while passing extrinsic state at call time is exactly Flyweight
rationale: Shared intrinsic data plus client-supplied extrinsic state at call time is the correct Flyweight design.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: In this module, the main goal of the Flyweight pattern is to ___.
optionB: decouple an abstraction from its implementation
optionC: provide a controlled surrogate for a costly object
optionD: support very large numbers of similar objects efficiently by sharing their common state
correctAnswerText: support very large numbers of similar objects efficiently by sharing their common state
rationale: Flyweight reduces memory by sharing common (intrinsic) state across many fine-grained objects. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In a Flyweight design, where do intrinsic and extrinsic state respectively live?
optionA: Intrinsic state is shared and stored inside the flyweight; extrinsic state is context-dependent and supplied by the client at call time
optionB: Both intrinsic and extrinsic state are stored inside each shared flyweight
optionC: Both are passed in by the client on every call and never stored
optionD: Intrinsic state is per-client; extrinsic state is the shared part
correctAnswerText: Intrinsic state is shared and stored inside the flyweight; extrinsic state is context-dependent and supplied by the client at call time
rationale: Intrinsic (shared) state lives in the flyweight; extrinsic (context) state must be supplied by the client per operation so it is not duplicated across the shared instances.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A tile-based game draws millions of map cells drawn from a handful of terrain kinds; each kind’s sprite and movement-cost repeat across cells, while each cell’s grid coordinates differ. How do you avoid storing the sprite data once per cell?
optionA: Build a tree of cells treated uniformly
optionB: Keep one shared object per terrain kind holding the repeating data, and pass each cell’s coordinates in at draw time
optionC: Wrap each cell in a behaviour-adding layer
optionD: Give each cell a surrogate that controls access
correctAnswerText: Keep one shared object per terrain kind holding the repeating data, and pass each cell’s coordinates in at draw time
rationale: Sharing the repeating intrinsic data per terrain kind while passing per-cell position (extrinsic) at call time is Flyweight. The distractors are Composite, Decorator, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Prototype, because new particles are produced
optionB: Flyweight, because a pool hands out shared instances keyed by intrinsic state while per-call context (position, velocity) is passed in
optionC: Singleton, because the pool is shared
optionD: Proxy, because get() stands in for a particle
correctAnswerText: Flyweight, because a pool hands out shared instances keyed by intrinsic state while per-call context (position, velocity) is passed in
rationale: ParticlePool returns shared instances keyed by intrinsic data (color/sprite) and the per-use position/velocity is passed to draw() as extrinsic state — Flyweight. It is not cloning (Prototype), a single global instance (Singleton), or access control (Proxy).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A design described as Flyweight stores each glyph’s on-screen x/y as a member field and creates a brand-new object for every character on the page, sharing nothing. Does this achieve Flyweight’s goal?
optionA: Yes — a Flyweight is supposed to store all state internally
optionB: Yes — one object per character is the intended outcome
optionC: No — keeping per-instance extrinsic state (position) and never sharing instances negates Flyweight; position should be extrinsic and instances shared
optionD: No — because Flyweight is not allowed to use a factory or pool
correctAnswerText: No — keeping per-instance extrinsic state (position) and never sharing instances negates Flyweight; position should be extrinsic and instances shared
rationale: Flyweight’s savings come from sharing instances and externalising context state. Storing each glyph’s position internally and creating one object per character defeats the purpose. A factory/pool is in fact a normal part of Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Proxy pattern?
optionB: Add behaviour by stacking wrappers
optionC: Provide a surrogate or placeholder that controls access to another object
correctAnswerText: Provide a surrogate or placeholder that controls access to another object
rationale: Proxy supplies a stand-in that controls access to a real subject. The distractors are Adapter, Decorator, and Composite.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: How does a proxy typically relate to its real subject?
optionA: It exposes a different interface than the subject
optionB: It inherits the subject’s private data directly
optionC: It replaces the subject permanently
optionD: It implements the same interface as the subject and forwards requests, optionally adding access control
correctAnswerText: It implements the same interface as the subject and forwards requests, optionally adding access control
rationale: A proxy shares the subject’s interface so it is interchangeable, forwarding calls while controlling access (lazy load, protection, remoting).
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A large image is expensive to load; you want a stand-in that exposes the same interface but defers loading until the image is actually drawn. Which pattern?
optionB: Decorator
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Proxy
rationale: A same-interface placeholder that delays creation of the real object is a (virtual) Proxy. The distractors add behaviour, convert, or build trees.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Image { public: virtual void draw() = 0; };  class ProxyImage : public Image { RealImage* real = nullptr; std::string file; public: void draw() override { if (!real) real = new RealImage(file); real->draw(); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Flyweight
correctAnswerText: Proxy
rationale: Same-interface wrapper that lazily creates and delegates to the real subject is a virtual Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A wrapper implements the subject’s interface and adds logging plus access checks before delegating, without changing the operation’s result or adding new client-facing features. Best classified as?
optionA: Decorator — any wrapper that does extra work is a Decorator
optionB: Adapter — it converts the interface
optionC: Proxy — it controls access to the same operation without adding new client-facing behaviour
correctAnswerText: Proxy — it controls access to the same operation without adding new client-facing behaviour
rationale: Controlling access (auth/logging) to the same operation, without new client-facing responsibilities, is Proxy intent, distinguishing it from Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: Proxy’s core purpose is to ___.
optionA: share state among many fine-grained objects
optionB: define a family of interchangeable algorithms
optionC: simplify a subsystem behind one interface
optionD: act as a controlled stand-in for another object
correctAnswerText: act as a controlled stand-in for another object
rationale: Proxy is a controlled surrogate for another object. The distractors are Flyweight, Strategy, and Facade.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: What distinguishes a Proxy from a Decorator in intent?
optionA: A Proxy controls or manages access to the subject, while a Decorator adds new responsibilities
optionB: They are identical in intent
optionC: A Proxy always adds new features
optionD: A Decorator changes the object’s interface
correctAnswerText: A Proxy controls or manages access to the subject, while a Decorator adds new responsibilities
rationale: Both wrap a same-interface object, but Proxy’s intent is access control/management whereas Decorator’s is adding behaviour.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Remote service calls are costly; you want a local object with the same interface that transparently handles the connection and caches results. Which pattern?
optionB: Proxy
optionC: Adapter
correctAnswerText: Proxy
rationale: A same-interface local stand-in for a remote object (managing connection/caching) is a remote Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class BankAccount { public: virtual void withdraw(int) = 0; };  class ProtectedAccount : public BankAccount { RealAccount acc; User u; public: void withdraw(int n) override { if (u.isOwner()) acc.withdraw(n); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: Proxy
correctAnswerText: Proxy
rationale: A same-interface wrapper that performs an access check before delegating is a protection Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class wraps a Database with the same interface and simply forwards every call unchanged — no access control, lazy initialization, or remoting. Is “Proxy” the right label?
optionA: Yes — any same-interface wrapper is a Proxy
optionB: Yes — forwarding is the whole point
optionC: No — Proxy requires changing the interface
optionD: No — with no access control, lazy loading, or remoting it serves no proxy purpose
correctAnswerText: No — with no access control, lazy loading, or remoting it serves no proxy purpose
rationale: A pure pass-through adds none of the access-management responsibilities that define a Proxy, so the label is not meaningful here.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Structural module content

prompt: The defining purpose of a Proxy is to ___.
optionB: decouple an abstraction from its implementation
optionC: provide a same-interface surrogate that controls access to a real object
optionD: convert one interface into another the caller expects
correctAnswerText: provide a same-interface surrogate that controls access to a real object
rationale: Proxy supplies a stand-in that controls access to the real subject. The distractors are Composite, Bridge, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Why must a proxy expose the same interface as the real subject it guards?
optionA: So it can be substituted for the subject transparently while it decides whether and when to forward calls
optionB: So it can offer the caller brand-new operations the subject lacks
optionC: So it can translate the subject into an unrelated interface
optionD: So that it can store all of the subject’s state internally
correctAnswerText: So it can be substituted for the subject transparently while it decides whether and when to forward calls
rationale: Sharing the subject’s interface lets the proxy be used wherever the subject is, transparently controlling access (lazy load, auth, remoting). Adding new operations would be Decorator; translating would be Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A reporting screen references thousands of high-resolution attachments, but most are never opened. You want each attachment reference to behave exactly like the real document object yet defer the costly disk read until something actually calls render(). What design fits?
optionA: A wrapper that adds new export buttons to the document
optionB: A same-interface placeholder that loads the real document only on first use
optionC: A class converting the document into a printer interface
optionD: A shared pool that stores one document per file type
correctAnswerText: A same-interface placeholder that loads the real document only on first use
rationale: A same-interface stand-in that postpones creating/loading the real object until it is used is a virtual Proxy. The distractors are Decorator, Adapter, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Decorator, because it does extra work before forwarding
optionB: Adapter, because it wraps another object
optionC: Composite, because it manages stored slots
optionD: Proxy, because it shares the subject’s interface and gates access without adding new client-facing operations
correctAnswerText: Proxy, because it shares the subject’s interface and gates access without adding new client-facing operations
rationale: GuardedVault keeps the same Vault interface and performs an access check before delegating, adding no new client-facing feature — a protection Proxy. Decorator would add a visible new responsibility; Adapter would convert the interface.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class wraps a RemoteLedger with an identical interface but does nothing except call through to it on every method — no lazy loading, no caching, no access checks, no remoting setup. Is “Proxy” a meaningful classification here?
optionA: Yes — any same-interface pass-through is a Proxy
optionB: Yes — forwarding alone fulfils Proxy’s intent
optionC: No — a Proxy must convert the subject’s interface, which this does not
optionD: No — without any access management (lazy load, caching, protection, remoting) it serves no proxy purpose
correctAnswerText: No — without any access management (lazy load, caching, protection, remoting) it serves no proxy purpose
rationale: Proxy is defined by some form of access control or management. A pure pass-through provides none, so the label is hollow. Proxy does not convert interfaces — that is Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the purpose of the Repository pattern?
optionA: Provide a collection-like interface that mediates between domain logic and data storage, hiding persistence details
optionB: Convert one interface into another
optionC: Ensure a class has one instance
correctAnswerText: Provide a collection-like interface that mediates between domain logic and data storage, hiding persistence details
rationale: Repository gives domain code a clean, collection-like interface over storage, hiding persistence. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Repository is NOT one of the original Gang-of-Four design patterns. How is it best classified?
optionA: As a GoF structural pattern like Adapter
optionB: As an enterprise/application architecture pattern for data access, outside the GoF catalog
optionC: As a GoF creational pattern
correctAnswerText: As an enterprise/application architecture pattern for data access, outside the GoF catalog
rationale: Repository comes from enterprise/application architecture (Fowler, Evans), not the GoF catalog; it is an application-architecture data-access pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Domain code should query and save User objects without knowing whether they live in SQL, a file, or an in-memory store, so the data source can be swapped freely. Which approach fits?
optionB: Decorator wrapping
optionC: A Repository abstraction over storage
correctAnswerText: A Repository abstraction over storage
rationale: A domain-facing interface that hides and allows swapping the storage mechanism is the Repository approach.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Examine:  class UserRepository { public: virtual User find(int id) = 0; virtual void save(User) = 0; };  class SqlUserRepository : public UserRepository { /* SQL */ };  class InMemoryUserRepository : public UserRepository { /* map */ };  What does this structure most directly demonstrate?
optionB: Access control (Proxy)
optionC: Algorithm selection (Strategy)
optionD: A Repository abstracting persistence behind a domain-facing interface
correctAnswerText: A Repository abstracting persistence behind a domain-facing interface
rationale: A domain-facing data-access interface with interchangeable backing stores is the Repository pattern (not GoF).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design
