prompt: A class named UserRepository builds SQL strings throughout the domain layer and exposes raw connection objects to callers. Does this honor the Repository idea?
optionA: No — Repository’s purpose is to hide persistence behind a clean interface; leaking SQL and connections to the domain defeats the separation
optionB: Yes — the name makes it a Repository
optionC: Yes — exposing connections is required
optionD: No — Repository must be a GoF pattern
correctAnswerText: No — Repository’s purpose is to hide persistence behind a clean interface; leaking SQL and connections to the domain defeats the separation
rationale: Repository exists to separate domain logic from data-access concerns; leaking SQL/connections breaks that separation regardless of the class name.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: A Repository primarily ___.
optionA: guarantees one instance of the database
optionB: separates domain logic from data-access concerns behind a collection-like interface
optionC: adds behaviour to objects by wrapping
optionD: traverses a collection without exposing it
correctAnswerText: separates domain logic from data-access concerns behind a collection-like interface
rationale: Repository separates domain logic from persistence behind a collection-like interface. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Which statement about Repository’s classification is accurate?
optionA: It is the GoF Bridge pattern under another name
optionB: It is a GoF structural pattern with automated detector support
optionC: It is a supplemental enterprise architecture pattern, not part of the GoF set, and has no automated GoF-style detector here
optionD: It is identical to the Proxy pattern
correctAnswerText: It is a supplemental enterprise architecture pattern, not part of the GoF set, and has no automated GoF-style detector here
rationale: Repository is an enterprise architecture pattern (not GoF), and CodiNeo provides no automated GoF-style detector for it — conceptual assessment only.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Tests must run against an in-memory data store while production uses a real database, with identical domain code. Which design enables this substitution cleanly?
optionB: A Decorator chain
optionC: A Facade
optionD: A Repository interface with interchangeable implementations
correctAnswerText: A Repository interface with interchangeable implementations
rationale: A Repository interface lets domain code stay the same while the backing store is swapped (in-memory for tests, DB for production).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Examine:  class OrderRepository { public: virtual std::optional<Order> byId(int) = 0; virtual std::vector<Order> all() = 0; virtual void add(Order) = 0; };  This domain-facing interface, with swappable backing stores, is an example of ___.
optionB: an Adapter converting interfaces
optionC: a Composite tree
correctAnswerText: a Repository abstracting data access
rationale: A collection-like, domain-facing interface decoupled from a concrete store is the Repository pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: A team adds a Repository interface, but every method returns raw database rows and requires callers to manage transactions and SQL. Is this a faithful Repository?
optionB: No — leaking raw rows, transactions, and SQL to callers breaks the abstraction the pattern exists to provide
optionC: Yes — as long as it is named Repository
optionD: No — Repositories may not return data at all
correctAnswerText: No — leaking raw rows, transactions, and SQL to callers breaks the abstraction the pattern exists to provide
rationale: Repository must insulate the domain from data-access mechanics; exposing rows/transactions/SQL defeats its purpose.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: A Repository’s main job is to ___.
optionA: provide a collection-like interface that separates domain logic from how entities are stored
optionB: ensure only one database connection ever exists
optionC: attach optional behaviour to entities by wrapping them
optionD: convert a storage interface into a UI interface
correctAnswerText: provide a collection-like interface that separates domain logic from how entities are stored
rationale: Repository gives domain code a clean, collection-like interface over persistence, hiding storage details. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Adapter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: How should Repository be classified relative to the original Gang-of-Four catalog?
optionA: As a GoF structural pattern, alongside Adapter and Proxy
optionB: As a GoF creational pattern for building entities
optionC: As a C++ language idiom like PIMPL
optionD: As a supplemental enterprise / application-architecture pattern for data access, outside the GoF set
correctAnswerText: As a supplemental enterprise / application-architecture pattern for data access, outside the GoF set
rationale: Repository originates in enterprise/application architecture (Fowler; Evans) and is not part of the GoF catalog; it is an application-architecture data-access pattern, not a GoF structural pattern or a language idiom.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: Your service code must create, fetch, and delete Invoice entities identically whether they live in PostgreSQL in production or an in-memory map during tests, with the service code never mentioning SQL or connections. Which approach achieves this cleanly?
optionA: Make the database a single global instance the service reads directly
optionB: Define an Invoice-storage interface (findById, save, delete) with interchangeable backing implementations behind it
optionC: Wrap each Invoice in a behaviour-adding layer
optionD: Put a simplified front over the application’s user interface
correctAnswerText: Define an Invoice-storage interface (findById, save, delete) with interchangeable backing implementations behind it
rationale: A domain-facing storage interface with swappable implementations (real DB vs in-memory) that keeps persistence details out of the domain is the Repository approach. The distractors are Singleton, Decorator, and Facade-over-UI.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

optionA: A Repository: a domain-facing, collection-like interface that hides and lets you swap the persistence mechanism
optionB: An Adapter converting one storage interface into another
optionC: A Proxy controlling access to the database
optionD: A Bridge varying two independent hierarchies
correctAnswerText: A Repository: a domain-facing, collection-like interface that hides and lets you swap the persistence mechanism
rationale: A domain-facing interface (findById/save) with interchangeable backing stores (SQL, in-memory) that conceals persistence is the Repository pattern — a non-GoF enterprise pattern. It is not interface conversion (Adapter), access control (Proxy), or two co-varying hierarchies (Bridge).
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: A class named OrderRepository exposes methods that return raw result-set cursors and require each caller to open transactions and assemble SQL fragments. Does this faithfully realise the Repository idea?
optionA: Yes — the name OrderRepository makes it a Repository
optionB: Yes — a Repository is supposed to surface SQL and connections
optionC: No — leaking cursors, transactions, and SQL to callers breaks the domain/persistence separation the pattern exists to provide
optionD: No — a Repository may not return any data to callers
correctAnswerText: No — leaking cursors, transactions, and SQL to callers breaks the domain/persistence separation the pattern exists to provide
rationale: Repository exists to insulate domain code from data-access mechanics. Exposing cursors, transactions, and SQL defeats that separation regardless of the class name. Returning entities is exactly what it should do — just not raw rows.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Structural module content; Fowler (2002), Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — Repository; Evans (2003), Domain-Driven Design

prompt: What is the intent of the Chain of Responsibility pattern?
optionA: Pass a request along a chain of handlers until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver
optionB: Convert interfaces
optionC: Ensure one instance
correctAnswerText: Pass a request along a chain of handlers until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver
rationale: Chain of Responsibility forwards a request through handlers until one handles it. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What does each handler in the chain decide?
optionB: Whether to handle the request itself or pass it to the next handler
optionC: How to convert the request’s interface
correctAnswerText: Whether to handle the request itself or pass it to the next handler
rationale: Each handler either handles the request or forwards it down the chain.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A support ticket should be tried by level-1, then level-2, then a manager, each handling it or passing it on, with the sender unaware who resolves it. Which pattern?
optionB: Command
optionC: Chain of Responsibility
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility
rationale: Sequential handle-or-forward with sender/receiver decoupling is Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Handler { protected: Handler* next = nullptr; public: void setNext(Handler* n) { next = n; } virtual void handle(int lvl) { if (next) next->handle(lvl); } };  class Warn : public Handler { void handle(int lvl) override { if (lvl < 2) log("warn"); else Handler::handle(lvl); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility
rationale: Handlers each holding a next link and choosing to handle or forward a request is Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Wrappers each hold a “next” and either consume a request or forward it; unlike a wrapper that always augments and delegates, a link may stop the request. Best classified as?
optionA: Chain of Responsibility — handlers conditionally handle or forward, possibly stopping the request
optionB: Decorator — any chain of wrappers is Decorator
optionC: Adapter — it converts requests
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility — handlers conditionally handle or forward, possibly stopping the request
rationale: Conditional handle-or-forward (with the option to stop) is Chain of Responsibility; a Decorator always delegates while adding behaviour.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Chain of Responsibility ___.
optionB: lets a request travel through a series of handlers until one deals with it
optionC: shares intrinsic state across objects
correctAnswerText: lets a request travel through a series of handlers until one deals with it
rationale: Chain of Responsibility passes a request through handlers until one handles it. The distractors are Memento, Flyweight, and Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What coupling does Chain of Responsibility reduce?
optionB: Between leaf and composite
optionC: Between the request’s sender and the specific receiver, since the sender need not know which handler responds
correctAnswerText: Between the request’s sender and the specific receiver, since the sender need not know which handler responds
rationale: The sender is decoupled from the concrete handler that ends up servicing the request.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An HTTP request passes through authentication, then rate-limiting, then logging middleware; any stage may handle/reject it or pass it on. Which pattern?
optionB: Facade
optionC: Template Method
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility
rationale: A pipeline of handlers that each handle-or-forward a request is Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Approver { protected: Approver* next = nullptr; public: void setNext(Approver* n) { next = n; } virtual void approve(int amt) { if (next) next->approve(amt); } };  class Manager : public Approver { void approve(int amt) override { if (amt <= 1000) sign(); else Approver::approve(amt); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Command
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility
rationale: Linked approvers each handling within their limit or forwarding to the next is Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Each validator holds a reference to the next; on failure it stops, otherwise it calls the next, and the caller doesn’t know which validator ran. Is “Chain of Responsibility” correct?
optionB: Yes — linked handlers that each handle-or-pass while hiding the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility
optionC: No — the sender must know the handler
optionD: Yes — but only with one handler
correctAnswerText: Yes — linked handlers that each handle-or-pass while hiding the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility
rationale: Linked handlers that each handle-or-forward while the sender stays unaware of the receiver is Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In the Chain of Responsibility pattern, a request ___.
optionA: is snapshotted so it can be restored later
optionB: travels along a series of handlers until one of them handles it
optionC: is broadcast to every registered dependent at once
optionD: is evaluated as a tree of grammar rules
correctAnswerText: travels along a series of handlers until one of them handles it
rationale: Chain of Responsibility forwards a request through handlers until one handles it, decoupling sender from receiver. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Interpreter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What decision does each handler in the chain make when it receives a request?
optionB: How to convert the request’s interface
optionC: Whether to handle the request itself or pass it on to its successor
optionD: Which single shared instance to return
correctAnswerText: Whether to handle the request itself or pass it on to its successor
rationale: Each handler either handles the request or forwards it to the next handler in the chain.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An expense claim should be tried by a team lead, then a department head, then finance, each either approving within its limit or passing it on, with the submitter unaware of who ultimately approves. Which approach fits?
optionA: Coordinate the approvers through a central hub that routes the claim
optionB: Link the approvers so each handles the claim within its limit or forwards it to the next
optionC: Notify all approvers simultaneously and let each react
optionD: Snapshot the claim before each approval step
correctAnswerText: Link the approvers so each handles the claim within its limit or forwards it to the next
rationale: Linked handlers that each handle-or-forward while hiding the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility. The distractors describe Mediator, Observer, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Decorator — a wrapper that always augments and delegates
optionB: Composite — a part-whole tree
optionC: Observer — a subject notifying dependents
optionD: Chain of Responsibility — linked handlers that each handle or forward, possibly stopping the request
correctAnswerText: Chain of Responsibility — linked handlers that each handle or forward, possibly stopping the request
rationale: Each handler holds a successor and chooses to consume (stop) or forward the request — Chain of Responsibility, distinct from a Decorator that always delegates.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Each link holds a reference to the next and either stops the request on a condition or calls the next, and the caller never learns which link acted. A teammate calls this Decorator. Is that defensible?
optionA: No — a link that may stop the request and hides the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility, not a Decorator that always delegates while adding behaviour
optionB: Yes — any chain of wrappers holding a next reference is Decorator
optionC: Yes — because both forward to a successor
optionD: No — but only because Decorator requires inheritance
correctAnswerText: No — a link that may stop the request and hides the receiver from the sender is Chain of Responsibility, not a Decorator that always delegates while adding behaviour
rationale: Conditional handle-or-stop with sender/receiver decoupling is Chain of Responsibility; a Decorator always delegates while augmenting, so the label is wrong.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Command pattern?
optionB: Share intrinsic state
optionC: Encapsulate a request as an object, so you can parameterise, queue, log, and undo operations
correctAnswerText: Encapsulate a request as an object, so you can parameterise, queue, log, and undo operations
rationale: Command reifies a request as an object enabling queuing, logging, and undo. The distractors are Adapter, Flyweight, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What does turning a request into a Command object enable?
optionB: Automatic multithreading
optionC: Removing the receiver
optionD: Storing, queuing, and undoing operations uniformly, and decoupling the invoker from the receiver
correctAnswerText: Storing, queuing, and undoing operations uniformly, and decoupling the invoker from the receiver
rationale: A reified request can be stored, queued, replayed, and undone, and it decouples the invoker from the concrete receiver.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A text editor needs undo/redo and a macro recorder; each user action must be storable, replayable, and reversible. Which pattern?
optionB: Observer
optionC: Strategy
correctAnswerText: Command
rationale: Reifying actions so they can be stored, replayed, and undone is Command. The distractors notify, swap algorithms, or traverse.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Command { public: virtual void execute() = 0; virtual void undo() = 0; };  class InsertText : public Command { Doc& d; std::string t; public: void execute() override { d.insert(t); } void undo() override { d.erase(t); } };  class Invoker { std::vector<Command*> history; public: void run(Command* c) { c->execute(); history.push_back(c); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Command
rationale: Reified requests with execute/undo, run and recorded by an invoker, is the Command pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A design calls receiver methods directly from the UI handler with no request object, yet claims to support undo by re-reading the UI. Is this the Command pattern?
optionA: Yes — any action with undo is Command
optionB: Yes — direct calls are Command
optionC: No — Command requires reifying each request as an object (with execute/undo); calling the receiver directly is not Command
correctAnswerText: No — Command requires reifying each request as an object (with execute/undo); calling the receiver directly is not Command
rationale: Command’s essence is making each request a first-class object; direct receiver calls without command objects are not Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: The Command pattern ___.
optionB: wraps an action and its parameters in an object so it can be executed later, queued, or undone
optionC: defines a one-to-many dependency
correctAnswerText: wraps an action and its parameters in an object so it can be executed later, queued, or undone
rationale: Command wraps an action as an object for later execution, queuing, or undo. The distractors are Flyweight, Observer, and Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does Command decouple the invoker from the receiver?
optionA: The invoker triggers an abstract command and never needs to know which receiver or operation it wraps
optionB: The invoker inherits from the receiver
optionC: The receiver calls the invoker directly
optionD: They must be the same object
correctAnswerText: The invoker triggers an abstract command and never needs to know which receiver or operation it wraps
rationale: The invoker depends only on the abstract command, so it is independent of the concrete receiver and operation.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A home-automation remote must map each button to a configurable action, support queuing scheduled actions, and undo the last one. Which pattern?
optionB: Command
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: Command
rationale: Configurable, queueable, undoable actions as objects is Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Operation { public: virtual void execute() = 0; };  class TurnOnLight : public Operation { Light& l; public: TurnOnLight(Light& x) : l(x) {} void execute() override { l.on(); } };  class Button { Operation* op; public: void press() { op->execute(); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Strategy
optionC: Command
correctAnswerText: Command
rationale: An action reified as an object that an invoker (Button) triggers without knowing the receiver is Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Each user action is an object with execute() and undo(), stored in a history stack the invoker pops to reverse actions. Is “Command” correct?
optionB: No — Command cannot support undo
optionC: Yes — but only without a history
optionD: Yes — reifying requests as execute/undo objects managed via a history is exactly Command
correctAnswerText: Yes — reifying requests as execute/undo objects managed via a history is exactly Command
rationale: Reified requests with execute/undo and a history for reversal is the Command pattern (Memento stores state, not requests).
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Command pattern turns ___.
optionA: a collection into a sequentially accessible sequence
optionB: a request into an object so it can be stored, queued, replayed, or undone
optionC: a grammar rule into an evaluable class
optionD: an object’s behaviour into a function of its internal state
correctAnswerText: a request into an object so it can be stored, queued, replayed, or undone
rationale: Command reifies a request as an object enabling storage, queuing, replay, and undo. The distractors describe Iterator, Interpreter, and State.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What does packaging an action as a Command object let the invoker do that calling the receiver directly does not?
optionB: Convert the receiver’s interface to another
optionC: Store, queue, replay, and reverse the action while staying unaware of the concrete receiver
optionD: Notify a list of dependents automatically
correctAnswerText: Store, queue, replay, and reverse the action while staying unaware of the concrete receiver
rationale: A reified request can be stored, queued, replayed, and undone, and it decouples the invoker from the concrete receiver and operation.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A graphics editor must record each user action so the program can replay a recorded macro, queue scheduled actions, and reverse the most recent one. Which approach fits?
optionA: Wrap each action in an object exposing execute and undo, and keep them in a history list
optionB: Delegate each action to a strategy chosen by the user
optionC: Pass each action through a chain of handlers
optionD: Snapshot the whole document after every action
correctAnswerText: Wrap each action in an object exposing execute and undo, and keep them in a history list
rationale: Reifying each action as an execute/undo object held in a history is Command. The distractors describe Strategy, Chain of Responsibility, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Memento — it snapshots state for restore
optionB: State — behaviour changes with internal mode
optionC: Strategy — an interchangeable algorithm
optionD: Command — requests reified with run/revert and recorded by an invoker
correctAnswerText: Command — requests reified with run/revert and recorded by an invoker
rationale: Reified requests carrying run/revert, performed and recorded by an invoker that never names the receiver, is the Command pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A UI handler calls receiver methods directly inline and claims to support undo by re-reading the current widget values afterwards; there are no request objects. Does this implement Command?
optionA: No — Command requires each request to be a first-class object with execute/undo; direct inline calls are not Command
optionB: Yes — any feature offering undo is Command
optionC: Yes — calling the receiver directly is the standard Command form
optionD: No — Command forbids having any invoker at all
correctAnswerText: No — Command requires each request to be a first-class object with execute/undo; direct inline calls are not Command
rationale: Command’s essence is making each request a first-class object; calling the receiver directly without command objects is not the pattern.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Interpreter pattern?
optionB: Share intrinsic state
optionC: Given a language, define a representation for its grammar and an interpreter that uses the representation to evaluate sentences
correctAnswerText: Given a language, define a representation for its grammar and an interpreter that uses the representation to evaluate sentences
rationale: Interpreter represents a grammar and evaluates sentences in that language. The distractors are Adapter, Flyweight, and Proxy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How are grammar rules typically represented in the Interpreter pattern?
optionB: As a global lookup table only
optionC: As one giant switch statement
optionD: Each grammar rule becomes a class with an interpret() method; expressions form a tree of these objects
correctAnswerText: Each grammar rule becomes a class with an interpret() method; expressions form a tree of these objects
rationale: Each rule maps to a class with interpret(); a sentence is an abstract syntax tree of those rule objects.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: You must evaluate simple boolean expressions like (A AND B) OR C entered by users, composing rules recursively. Which pattern represents and evaluates such a grammar?
optionB: Visitor
optionC: Strategy
correctAnswerText: Interpreter
rationale: Representing grammar rules as composable evaluable objects is Interpreter. The distractors add operations, swap algorithms, or only model part-whole trees.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Expr { public: virtual int eval() = 0; };  class Num : public Expr { int v; public: int eval() override { return v; } };  class Add : public Expr { Expr* l; Expr* r; public: int eval() override { return l->eval() + r->eval(); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Interpreter
optionC: Iterator
correctAnswerText: Interpreter
rationale: Grammar-rule classes with eval() composed into an expression tree is the Interpreter pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A team uses the Interpreter pattern for a complex, performance-critical, full programming language with deeply nested grammar. Is this an appropriate use, and why?
optionA: Yes — Interpreter scales best to large languages
optionB: Yes — performance is Interpreter’s strength
optionC: No — Interpreter fits small, simple, stable grammars; large complex ones are better served by parser/compiler tools
correctAnswerText: No — Interpreter fits small, simple, stable grammars; large complex ones are better served by parser/compiler tools
rationale: Interpreter suits small, stable grammars; for large/complex performance-critical languages it becomes unwieldy and a parser/compiler is preferable.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: The Interpreter pattern ___.
optionB: selects interchangeable algorithms
optionC: captures and restores state
optionD: defines classes for a grammar’s rules and interprets sentences built from them
correctAnswerText: defines classes for a grammar’s rules and interprets sentences built from them
rationale: Interpreter defines grammar-rule classes and interprets sentences. The distractors are Observer, Strategy, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What is the relationship between Interpreter and a tree of expression objects?
optionA: Sentences are represented as an abstract syntax tree of rule objects, and interpretation recurses over that tree
optionB: Interpretation requires no data structure
optionC: Each sentence is one flat object
optionD: The grammar must be stored in a database
correctAnswerText: Sentences are represented as an abstract syntax tree of rule objects, and interpretation recurses over that tree
rationale: A sentence becomes an AST of rule objects; interpret() recurses over the tree to evaluate it.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A spreadsheet must parse and evaluate user formulas combining numbers, +, and *, nesting arbitrarily. Which pattern represents each operator/operand as an evaluable node?
optionB: Interpreter
optionC: Mediator
correctAnswerText: Interpreter
rationale: Representing each grammar element as a composable evaluable node is Interpreter.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Node { public: virtual bool eval(const Ctx&) = 0; };  class Var : public Node { std::string name; public: bool eval(const Ctx& c) override { return c.get(name); } };  class And : public Node { Node* a; Node* b; public: bool eval(const Ctx& c) override { return a->eval(c) && b->eval(c); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Strategy
optionC: Interpreter
correctAnswerText: Interpreter
rationale: Grammar-rule node classes with eval() composed into a tree is the Interpreter pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: For a tiny, rarely-changing query mini-language, a team models each rule as a class with eval() and composes them into a tree. Is Interpreter a reasonable choice here?
optionA: No — Interpreter never fits query languages
optionB: No — grammars cannot be trees
optionC: Yes — but only for arithmetic
optionD: Yes — Interpreter suits small, stable grammars represented as a tree of rule objects
correctAnswerText: Yes — Interpreter suits small, stable grammars represented as a tree of rule objects
rationale: A small, stable grammar represented as a tree of rule objects is exactly where Interpreter fits well.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Interpreter pattern represents a small language by ___.
optionA: snapshotting the parser’s state for undo
optionB: defining a class for each grammar rule and using them to evaluate sentences
optionC: broadcasting each token to a list of observers
optionD: wrapping each evaluation as an undoable command
correctAnswerText: defining a class for each grammar rule and using them to evaluate sentences
rationale: Interpreter models each grammar rule as a class and evaluates sentences built from them. The distractors describe Memento, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How is a sentence typically represented and evaluated in an Interpreter design?
optionA: As a single regular expression matched once
optionB: As one large switch statement over the raw text
optionC: As a tree of rule objects, with evaluation recursing over the tree by calling each node’s interpret
optionD: As a flat array of characters scanned left to right only
correctAnswerText: As a tree of rule objects, with evaluation recursing over the tree by calling each node’s interpret
rationale: A sentence becomes an abstract syntax tree of rule objects, and interpretation recurses over the tree, each node interpreting its children.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Users type small filter expressions such as price > 10 AND inStock that nest arbitrarily, and you must evaluate them by composing rules recursively. Which approach fits?
optionA: Model each operator and operand as a class with an interpret method and compose them into a tree
optionB: Add a new visitor for each kind of filter without changing the operand classes
optionC: Pick the evaluation algorithm from a menu at runtime
optionD: Pass the expression through a chain of handlers until one evaluates it
correctAnswerText: Model each operator and operand as a class with an interpret method and compose them into a tree
rationale: Representing each grammar element as a composable evaluable node and recursing is Interpreter. The distractors describe Visitor, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Composite — a structural part-whole tree with no evaluation semantics
optionB: Strategy — an interchangeable algorithm
optionC: Observer — a subject notifying dependents
optionD: Interpreter — grammar-rule classes with eval composed into an expression tree
correctAnswerText: Interpreter — grammar-rule classes with eval composed into an expression tree
rationale: Grammar-rule classes carrying eval and composed into an expression tree that evaluates a sentence is the Interpreter pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A team adopts the Interpreter pattern to implement a large, performance-critical general-purpose programming language with deeply nested grammar. Is this an appropriate use, and why?
optionA: No — Interpreter suits small, stable grammars; a large, complex, performance-critical language is better served by parser/compiler tooling
optionB: Yes — Interpreter scales best to large languages
optionC: Yes — raw evaluation speed is Interpreter’s main strength
optionD: No — Interpreter is unable to evaluate any expression
correctAnswerText: No — Interpreter suits small, stable grammars; a large, complex, performance-critical language is better served by parser/compiler tooling
rationale: Interpreter fits small, stable grammars; for large complex performance-critical languages the class-per-rule tree becomes unwieldy and a parser/compiler is preferable.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Iterator pattern?
optionA: Provide a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its underlying representation
optionB: Convert interfaces
optionC: Ensure one instance
correctAnswerText: Provide a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its underlying representation
rationale: Iterator gives sequential access while hiding the collection’s internals. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Decorator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Why use an Iterator instead of exposing the collection’s internal structure?
optionA: It makes the collection a Singleton
optionB: It lets clients traverse uniformly while the collection keeps its representation private and can change it
optionC: It converts the element type
optionD: It deletes the collection after traversal
correctAnswerText: It lets clients traverse uniformly while the collection keeps its representation private and can change it
rationale: Iterator decouples traversal from representation, so the collection can hide and later change its internals.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: You want client code to walk over a custom tree and a custom hash set the same way (begin/next/done) without knowing how each stores data. Which pattern?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Iterator
correctAnswerText: Iterator
rationale: Uniform sequential traversal that hides each collection’s internal storage is Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Iterator { public: virtual bool hasNext() = 0; virtual int next() = 0; };  class ListIterator : public Iterator { std::vector<int>& v; size_t i = 0; public: bool hasNext() override { return i < v.size(); } int next() override { return v[i++]; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Iterator
rationale: An object exposing hasNext/next while tracking position and hiding the container is the Iterator pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A “traversal” design forces clients to read the collection’s internal array index and bounds directly to loop. Does this satisfy Iterator’s intent?
optionA: No — exposing internal representation to clients defeats Iterator’s purpose
optionB: Yes — direct index access is the Iterator pattern
optionC: Yes — as long as there is a loop
correctAnswerText: No — exposing internal representation to clients defeats Iterator’s purpose
rationale: Iterator exists to hide the representation; making clients use internal indices breaks the encapsulation it provides.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Iterator provides ___.
optionB: sequential access to a collection’s elements without revealing its internal structure
optionC: a one-to-many notification
correctAnswerText: sequential access to a collection’s elements without revealing its internal structure
rationale: Iterator gives sequential element access while hiding internals. The distractors are Singleton, Observer, and Facade.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What responsibility moves into the iterator object?
optionB: Converting element interfaces
optionC: Tracking the current traversal position, so multiple independent traversals can coexist
correctAnswerText: Tracking the current traversal position, so multiple independent traversals can coexist
rationale: The iterator holds the traversal cursor, which allows several simultaneous, independent traversals of one collection.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A library exposes several container types; users should loop through any of them with the same code, and even run two simultaneous passes over one container. Which pattern?
optionB: Facade
optionC: Bridge
correctAnswerText: Iterator
rationale: Uniform traversal plus independent concurrent passes (separate cursors) is Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class TreeIterator { std::stack<Node*> st; public: TreeIterator(Node* root) { if (root) st.push(root); } bool done() { return st.empty(); } Node* current() { return st.top(); } void advance() { Node* n = st.top(); st.pop(); if (n->right) st.push(n->right); if (n->left) st.push(n->left); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Visitor
optionC: Composite
correctAnswerText: Iterator
rationale: An object that maintains traversal state and exposes done/current/advance over a structure is an Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A collection returns a cursor object exposing hasNext()/next() and keeps its storage (array vs linked list) private and swappable. Is “Iterator” a defensible label?
optionB: Yes — a cursor giving sequential access while the collection hides its representation is exactly Iterator
optionC: No — Iterator must expose the storage
correctAnswerText: Yes — a cursor giving sequential access while the collection hides its representation is exactly Iterator
rationale: A cursor giving sequential access while the underlying storage stays private and changeable is the Iterator pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Iterator pattern lets a client ___.
optionA: walk a collection’s elements one by one without seeing how the collection stores them
optionB: centralise communication between objects
optionC: restore an object to a previous state
optionD: wrap an action so it can be undone
correctAnswerText: walk a collection’s elements one by one without seeing how the collection stores them
rationale: Iterator provides sequential access while hiding the collection’s representation. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the benefit of moving the traversal cursor into a separate iterator object instead of exposing the collection’s internal array?
optionA: It forces the collection to become a single shared instance
optionB: It converts each element to a new type during the walk
optionC: It lets the collection notify dependents on every step
optionD: The collection can keep its storage private and even change it, while clients still traverse uniformly
correctAnswerText: The collection can keep its storage private and even change it, while clients still traverse uniformly
rationale: A separate cursor decouples traversal from representation, so the collection hides and can later change its internals while clients traverse uniformly.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A library exposes a ring buffer and a skip list; users should loop over either with identical code and even run two independent passes over the same container at once, without knowing the storage. Which approach fits?
optionA: Give each container a hub that coordinates who reads next
optionB: Snapshot the container before each read so it can be rolled back
optionC: Hand out cursor objects that track position and expose a uniform advance/done interface
optionD: Add a visitor that defines a new operation per element type
correctAnswerText: Hand out cursor objects that track position and expose a uniform advance/done interface
rationale: Uniform sequential access via position-tracking cursors that hide storage and allow concurrent passes is Iterator. The distractors describe Mediator, Memento, and Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionB: State — behaviour driven by internal mode
optionC: Command — a reified request
optionD: Iterator — an object that tracks position and exposes done/value/advance
correctAnswerText: Iterator — an object that tracks position and exposes done/value/advance
rationale: An object that holds the traversal position and exposes done/value/advance, hiding the underlying sequence, is an Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A so-called traversal API requires callers to read the collection’s public head pointer and node links directly, chasing them manually to loop. Does this honour the Iterator pattern’s intent?
optionA: No — exposing the internal representation to callers defeats the encapsulation Iterator exists to provide
optionB: Yes — manual pointer chasing is the canonical Iterator
optionC: Yes — any loop over elements counts as Iterator
optionD: No — Iterator prohibits sequential access entirely
correctAnswerText: No — exposing the internal representation to callers defeats the encapsulation Iterator exists to provide
rationale: Iterator exists precisely to hide the representation; forcing callers to use internal links breaks the encapsulation it is meant to preserve.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Mediator pattern?
optionB: Traverse a collection
optionC: Define an object that centralises and coordinates communication between a set of objects so they don’t refer to each other directly
correctAnswerText: Define an object that centralises and coordinates communication between a set of objects so they don’t refer to each other directly
rationale: Mediator centralises inter-object communication. The distractors are Adapter, Iterator, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does a Mediator reduce coupling among colleague objects?
optionA: By merging all colleagues into one class
optionB: By making each colleague a Singleton
optionC: By converting their interfaces
optionD: Colleagues talk only to the mediator, which routes interactions, instead of holding references to each other
correctAnswerText: Colleagues talk only to the mediator, which routes interactions, instead of holding references to each other
rationale: Colleagues communicate through the mediator rather than referencing each other, cutting many-to-many coupling.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A dialog’s many widgets (list, text box, button) must enable/disable each other in complex ways; you want to avoid each widget referencing all the others. Which pattern?
optionB: Observer
optionC: Composite
correctAnswerText: Mediator
rationale: Centralising the widgets’ mutual interactions in one coordinator is Mediator. Observer would be one-way notification.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Mediator { public: virtual void notify(Widget* sender, std::string ev) = 0; };  class Dialog : public Mediator { Button* ok; ListBox* list; public: void notify(Widget* s, std::string ev) override { if (s == list && ev == "select") ok->enable(); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: Mediator
rationale: A hub that receives events from colleagues and coordinates how others respond is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A hub receives events from peer objects and tells other peers how to react, governing their mutual interaction (not one-way notification, not just simplifying a subsystem). Best classified as?
optionB: Facade — it just simplifies a subsystem
optionC: Mediator — it centralises and coordinates mutual communication among peers
correctAnswerText: Mediator — it centralises and coordinates mutual communication among peers
rationale: Two-way coordination of peers’ mutual interactions is Mediator; Observer is one-way, Facade is one-way subsystem simplification.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: A Mediator object ___.
optionB: defines interchangeable algorithms
optionC: passes a request along a chain
optionD: coordinates interactions among objects so they need not reference each other directly
correctAnswerText: coordinates interactions among objects so they need not reference each other directly
rationale: Mediator coordinates interactions so colleagues stay decoupled. The distractors are Memento, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What problem does Mediator address as the number of interacting objects grows?
optionA: The explosion of direct many-to-many references, which it replaces with a single coordinating hub
optionB: Slow algorithms, replaced by faster ones
optionC: Excess memory, solved by sharing state
correctAnswerText: The explosion of direct many-to-many references, which it replaces with a single coordinating hub
rationale: Mediator replaces tangled many-to-many references with communication through one coordinator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In an air-traffic system, planes must not talk to each other directly; a control tower coordinates who lands when. Which pattern does the tower embody?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Strategy
correctAnswerText: Mediator
rationale: A central coordinator through which otherwise-decoupled peers interact is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class ChatRoom { std::vector<User*> users; public: void send(std::string msg, User* from) { for (auto* u : users) if (u != from) u->receive(msg); } };  class User { ChatRoom* room; public: void post(std::string m) { room->send(m, this); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: Mediator
correctAnswerText: Mediator
rationale: Users communicating only through a central ChatRoom that routes messages is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Form fields never reference each other; instead each notifies a FormController that decides how the other fields update. Is “Mediator” the right classification?
optionB: No — Mediator forbids more than two peers
optionC: Yes — but only for UI code
optionD: Yes — a central object coordinating mutual interactions among otherwise-decoupled peers is exactly Mediator
correctAnswerText: Yes — a central object coordinating mutual interactions among otherwise-decoupled peers is exactly Mediator
rationale: A central coordinator governing peers’ mutual interactions is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: According to this module, a Mediator object ___.
optionA: evaluates sentences of a small grammar
optionB: gives sequential access to a collection
optionC: captures an object’s state for restore
optionD: centralises and coordinates communication among a set of objects so they need not reference each other directly
correctAnswerText: centralises and coordinates communication among a set of objects so they need not reference each other directly
rationale: Mediator centralises inter-object communication so colleagues stay decoupled. The distractors describe Interpreter, Iterator, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How does a Mediator reduce coupling among its colleague objects?
optionA: By merging all colleagues into a single class
optionB: By converting each colleague’s interface to a common one
optionC: Colleagues talk only to the mediator, which routes interactions, instead of holding references to one another
optionD: By making each colleague a single shared instance
correctAnswerText: Colleagues talk only to the mediator, which routes interactions, instead of holding references to one another
rationale: Colleagues communicate through the mediator rather than referencing each other, replacing tangled many-to-many links with one hub.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In a booking form, selecting a country must refilter the city list, toggle a postcode field, and enable the submit button; you want to avoid each control holding references to all the others. Which approach fits?
optionA: Route every control’s events through one coordinator that decides how the others react
optionB: Have each control notify a list of dependents and update themselves
optionC: Pass each event through a chain of handlers until one acts
optionD: Give each control an interchangeable algorithm chosen at runtime
correctAnswerText: Route every control’s events through one coordinator that decides how the others react
rationale: Centralising the controls’ mutual interactions in one coordinator is Mediator; one-way notification would be Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Observer — a subject broadcasting to dependents
optionB: Mediator — a hub coordinating the mutual interaction of otherwise-decoupled peers
optionC: Facade — a simplified entry point to a subsystem
optionD: Visitor — operations added by double dispatch
correctAnswerText: Mediator — a hub coordinating the mutual interaction of otherwise-decoupled peers
rationale: Peers (planes) that never reference each other and interact only through a central coordinator that governs their mutual behaviour is Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A hub receives events from peer objects and instructs other peers how to respond, governing their two-way interaction — not merely forwarding one-way change notifications and not just exposing a simpler entry point to a subsystem. Best classified as?
optionA: Observer — it is one-to-many change notification
optionB: Facade — it only simplifies access to a subsystem
optionC: Adapter — it converts incompatible interfaces
optionD: Mediator — it centralises and coordinates the peers’ mutual communication
correctAnswerText: Mediator — it centralises and coordinates the peers’ mutual communication
rationale: Two-way coordination of peers’ mutual interactions is Mediator; Observer is one-way notification and Facade is one-way subsystem simplification.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Memento pattern?
optionA: Capture and externalise an object’s internal state so it can be restored later, without violating encapsulation
optionB: Convert interfaces
optionC: Ensure one instance
correctAnswerText: Capture and externalise an object’s internal state so it can be restored later, without violating encapsulation
rationale: Memento captures and restores state without breaking encapsulation. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does Memento preserve encapsulation while saving state?
optionB: The originator creates an opaque memento that the caretaker stores without inspecting its internals
optionC: It copies the originator’s class definition
optionD: It exposes the state through getters to everyone
correctAnswerText: The originator creates an opaque memento that the caretaker stores without inspecting its internals
rationale: The originator builds the memento; the caretaker stores it opaquely, never reading the internals, so encapsulation holds.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A drawing app needs unlimited undo by snapshotting the canvas state and restoring it later, without exposing the canvas’s private fields. Which pattern?
optionB: State
optionC: Memento
correctAnswerText: Memento
rationale: Snapshotting and restoring state without exposing internals is Memento. Command reifies requests; State models modes.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Memento { friend class Editor; std::string state; Memento(std::string s) : state(s) {} };  class Editor { std::string text; public: Memento save() { return Memento(text); } void restore(const Memento& m) { text = m.state; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: State
optionC: Prototype
correctAnswerText: Memento
rationale: An originator producing an opaque snapshot object it can later restore from is the Memento pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A “snapshot” design exposes all of an object’s internal fields publicly so external code can copy and later write them back. Does this satisfy Memento’s intent?
optionA: No — Memento must capture state without breaking encapsulation; exposing internals publicly violates that
optionB: Yes — public fields are required for Memento
optionC: Yes — any save/restore is Memento
correctAnswerText: No — Memento must capture state without breaking encapsulation; exposing internals publicly violates that
rationale: Memento’s point is capturing state without breaking encapsulation; publicly exposing internals defeats it.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Memento is used to ___.
optionB: save and later restore an object’s state without exposing its internal representation
optionC: define interchangeable algorithms
correctAnswerText: save and later restore an object’s state without exposing its internal representation
rationale: Memento saves and restores state without exposing internals. The distractors are Mediator, Strategy, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What are the three roles in the Memento pattern?
optionB: Context, State, Transition
optionC: Originator (owns state), Memento (stores a snapshot), and Caretaker (keeps mementos without inspecting them)
correctAnswerText: Originator (owns state), Memento (stores a snapshot), and Caretaker (keeps mementos without inspecting them)
rationale: Memento’s roles are Originator, Memento, and Caretaker; the caretaker stores snapshots opaquely.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A game must let players save a checkpoint and reload exactly that state later, while keeping the player object’s fields private. Which pattern?
optionB: Prototype
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Memento
rationale: Saving and restoring an object’s state without exposing its fields is Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Snapshot { friend class Game; int hp; int level; Snapshot(int h, int l) : hp(h), level(l) {} };  class Game { int hp; int level; public: Snapshot save() { return Snapshot(hp, level); } void load(const Snapshot& s) { hp = s.hp; level = s.level; } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: Memento
rationale: An originator producing/consuming an opaque state snapshot is the Memento pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An object returns an opaque token capturing its current state; a history manager stores tokens and hands one back to restore the object, never reading the token’s contents. Is “Memento” correct?
optionB: Yes — an opaque state object stored by a caretaker and used to restore the object, without inspection, is Memento
optionC: No — Memento requires public state
correctAnswerText: Yes — an opaque state object stored by a caretaker and used to restore the object, without inspection, is Memento
rationale: An opaque snapshot held by a caretaker and used to restore the originator, without inspection, is the Memento pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Memento pattern is used to ___.
optionA: coordinate communication among objects through a hub
optionB: select an algorithm at runtime
optionC: capture an object’s state so it can be restored later without exposing its internals
optionD: pass a request along a chain of handlers
correctAnswerText: capture an object’s state so it can be restored later without exposing its internals
rationale: Memento captures and restores state without breaking encapsulation. The distractors describe Mediator, Strategy, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How does the Memento pattern preserve encapsulation while saving an object’s state?
optionA: It makes all of the object’s fields public so anyone can copy them
optionB: The originator produces an opaque memento that the caretaker stores without reading its contents
optionC: It exposes the state through getters available to every caller
optionD: It copies the originator’s class definition at runtime
correctAnswerText: The originator produces an opaque memento that the caretaker stores without reading its contents
rationale: The originator builds the memento and the caretaker holds it opaquely, never inspecting the internals, so encapsulation is preserved.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A text editor must support unlimited undo by saving the buffer’s state at each step and restoring an earlier one on demand, without exposing the buffer’s private fields. Which approach fits?
optionA: Have the buffer return an opaque snapshot a history keeper stores and hands back to restore it
optionB: Wrap each edit as a command and replay it in reverse
optionC: Give the buffer a current-state object that changes its behaviour
optionD: Notify a list of dependents whenever the buffer changes
correctAnswerText: Have the buffer return an opaque snapshot a history keeper stores and hands back to restore it
rationale: Producing an opaque snapshot stored by a caretaker and used to restore the object, without exposing internals, is Memento. The distractors lean toward Command, State, and Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Command — a reified request with execute/undo
optionB: State — behaviour changes with internal mode
optionC: Prototype — cloning via a virtual copy
optionD: Memento — an originator producing/consuming an opaque state snapshot only it can read
correctAnswerText: Memento — an originator producing/consuming an opaque state snapshot only it can read
rationale: An originator emitting an opaque snapshot (whose internals only it can read via friendship) and restoring from it is the Memento pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A so-called snapshot design makes every internal field of an object public so external code can read them all, copy them, and later write them back directly. Does this honour Memento’s intent?
optionA: No — Memento captures state without breaking encapsulation; exposing all internals publicly violates that core intent
optionB: Yes — public fields are a requirement of Memento
optionC: Yes — any save-and-restore scheme is Memento
optionD: No — because Memento is unable to restore state at all
correctAnswerText: No — Memento captures state without breaking encapsulation; exposing all internals publicly violates that core intent
rationale: Memento exists to capture state while keeping internals hidden; making every field public defeats the encapsulation the pattern is meant to protect.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Observer pattern?
optionB: Share intrinsic state
optionC: Define a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, its dependents are notified automatically
optionD: Encapsulate a request as an object
correctAnswerText: Define a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, its dependents are notified automatically
rationale: Observer establishes one-to-many automatic notification. The distractors are Adapter, Flyweight, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How do observers learn about changes in the subject?
optionB: Observers inherit the subject’s data
optionC: Each observer edits the subject directly
optionD: The subject holds a list of observers and calls their update method when its state changes
correctAnswerText: The subject holds a list of observers and calls their update method when its state changes
rationale: The subject keeps registered observers and notifies them (calls update) on change; observers do not poll or inherit.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A spreadsheet cell’s value changes and several charts plus a status bar must refresh automatically, without the cell knowing their concrete types. Which pattern?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Command
correctAnswerText: Observer
rationale: Automatic one-to-many notification with loose coupling to dependents is Observer. The distractors coordinate peers, encapsulate requests, or swap algorithms.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Observer { public: virtual void update(int) = 0; };  class Subject { std::vector<Observer*> obs; int v; public: void attach(Observer* o) { obs.push_back(o); } void set(int x) { v = x; for (auto* o : obs) o->update(v); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Observer
optionC: Visitor
correctAnswerText: Observer
rationale: A subject maintaining observers and broadcasting state changes to them is the Observer pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Each dependent registers with one source and is notified when that source changes; the dependents do not coordinate each other’s mutual interactions. Best classified as?
optionB: Command — it encapsulates requests
optionC: Observer — it is one-to-many change notification, not centralised peer coordination
correctAnswerText: Observer — it is one-to-many change notification, not centralised peer coordination
rationale: One-to-many change notification is Observer; Mediator would centralise two-way coordination among peers.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Observer establishes ___.
optionB: an interchangeable-algorithm family
optionC: a request encapsulated as an object
optionD: a one-to-many notification relationship between a subject and its dependents
correctAnswerText: a one-to-many notification relationship between a subject and its dependents
rationale: Observer is one-to-many change notification. The distractors are Composite, Strategy, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Why does the subject depend only on an observer interface, not concrete observers?
optionA: So new observer types can subscribe without modifying the subject (loose coupling)
optionB: So the subject can delete observers’ data
optionC: Because observers must be Singletons
optionD: So the subject can convert their interfaces
correctAnswerText: So new observer types can subscribe without modifying the subject (loose coupling)
rationale: Depending on an abstract observer interface lets new observers subscribe without changing the subject — the loose coupling Observer provides.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: When a stock price updates, a dashboard, a logger, and an alert service must all react immediately, and more subscribers may be added later. Which pattern?
optionB: Observer
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: Observer
rationale: Dynamic, extensible one-to-many notification is Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Listener { public: virtual void onEvent(const Event&) = 0; };  class Button { std::vector<Listener*> ls; public: void addListener(Listener* l) { ls.push_back(l); } void click() { Event e; for (auto* l : ls) l->onEvent(e); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: Observer
rationale: A source maintaining listeners and broadcasting an event to all of them is Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A publisher keeps a subscriber list and, on each new message, loops calling subscriber.notify(msg); subscribers can join or leave at runtime. Is this a correct Observer?
optionA: No — Observer needs a central hub coordinating peers
optionB: No — subscribers cannot change at runtime
optionC: Yes — but only with one subscriber
optionD: Yes — maintaining subscribers and broadcasting state changes to them dynamically is exactly Observer
correctAnswerText: Yes — maintaining subscribers and broadcasting state changes to them dynamically is exactly Observer
rationale: Dynamic subscription plus broadcast notification of changes is the Observer pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Observer pattern sets up a relationship in which ___.
optionA: a request travels through handlers until one accepts it
optionB: one subject automatically notifies many dependents when its state changes
optionC: two abstractions vary independently
optionD: an object snapshots its state for later restore
correctAnswerText: one subject automatically notifies many dependents when its state changes
rationale: Observer establishes one-to-many automatic notification. The distractors describe Chain of Responsibility, Bridge, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How do dependents in an Observer design find out that the subject changed?
optionA: They periodically poll a shared global flag
optionB: They inherit the subject’s data members
optionC: The subject holds their references and calls each one’s update method when its state changes
optionD: They each rewrite the subject’s state directly
correctAnswerText: The subject holds their references and calls each one’s update method when its state changes
rationale: The subject keeps a list of registered observers and calls their update method on change; observers neither poll nor inherit.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: When a sensor reading updates, a live graph, an audit log, and a threshold alarm must all react immediately, the sensor must not know their concrete types, and new reactors may be added later. Which approach fits?
optionA: Route every reaction through a central coordinator that decides who reacts to whom
optionB: Have the sensor maintain a list of dependents and notify each when its reading changes
optionC: Pass the reading through a series of handlers until one consumes it
optionD: Wrap the reading update as an undoable command object
correctAnswerText: Have the sensor maintain a list of dependents and notify each when its reading changes
rationale: Loosely-coupled, extensible one-to-many notification triggered by the source is Observer. The distractors describe Mediator, Chain of Responsibility, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Mediator — a hub coordinates mutual interaction
optionB: Visitor — operations are added via double dispatch
optionC: Observer — the subject broadcasts state changes to registered dependents
correctAnswerText: Observer — the subject broadcasts state changes to registered dependents
rationale: Thermostat keeps a list of dependents and broadcasts each state change to all of them — the one-to-many notification of Observer.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Each dependent registers with a single source and is notified when that source changes; the dependents never coordinate one another’s mutual behaviour through a hub. Should this be classified as Observer or Mediator?
optionA: Mediator — any notification mechanism is Mediator
optionB: Command — it encapsulates requests as objects
optionC: State — the source changes behaviour with its state
optionD: Observer — it is one-way change notification from one source to many dependents, not centralised peer coordination
correctAnswerText: Observer — it is one-way change notification from one source to many dependents, not centralised peer coordination
rationale: One-way change notification from one subject to many dependents is Observer; Mediator would centralise two-way coordination among peers.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the State pattern?
optionB: Share intrinsic state
optionC: Allow an object to alter its behaviour when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
correctAnswerText: Allow an object to alter its behaviour when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
rationale: State lets an object change behaviour as its internal state changes. The distractors are Adapter, Flyweight, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does the State pattern avoid large conditional blocks on a status field?
optionA: By using one giant switch statement
optionB: By making the object a Singleton
optionC: By copying the object per state
optionD: By representing each state as an object and delegating behaviour to the current state, which can switch to another
correctAnswerText: By representing each state as an object and delegating behaviour to the current state, which can switch to another
rationale: Each state becomes an object encapsulating its behaviour and transitions; the context delegates to the current one.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A TCP connection behaves differently when Closed, Listening, or Established, and each event may move it to another mode with its own rules. Which pattern?
optionB: Strategy
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: State
rationale: Behaviour and transitions driven by the object’s changing mode is State. Strategy is client-chosen and not transition-driven.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class State { public: virtual void insertCoin(Machine&) = 0; };  class NoCoin : public State { void insertCoin(Machine& m) override { m.setState(new HasCoin()); } };  class Machine { State* s; public: void setState(State* x) { s = x; } void insertCoin() { s->insertCoin(*this); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: State
optionC: Command
correctAnswerText: State
rationale: Behaviour delegated to a current-state object that itself triggers the transition to another state is the State pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Behaviour is delegated to an interchangeable object, and that object itself decides to replace the current one based on events, driving transitions. Is this State or Strategy?
optionB: Observer — it notifies dependents
optionC: State — the behaviour objects drive transitions based on the object’s changing state
optionD: Template Method — it fixes a skeleton
correctAnswerText: State — the behaviour objects drive transitions based on the object’s changing state
rationale: When the delegated objects themselves drive transitions based on the object’s evolving state, it is State, not a client-chosen Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: The State pattern lets ___.
optionA: many observers be notified of changes
optionB: algorithms be chosen by the client
optionC: requests be encapsulated as objects
optionD: an object change its behaviour as its internal state changes, as if its class changed
correctAnswerText: an object change its behaviour as its internal state changes, as if its class changed
rationale: State changes behaviour as internal state changes. The distractors are Observer, Strategy, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What replaces sprawling if/switch-on-status logic in the State pattern?
optionA: Separate state classes that encapsulate state-specific behaviour and transitions
optionB: A single Singleton holding all logic
optionC: A strategy chosen once by the client
correctAnswerText: Separate state classes that encapsulate state-specific behaviour and transitions
rationale: Each state becomes its own class owning that state’s behaviour and transitions, removing the central conditional.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A document moves through Draft, Moderation, and Published, where allowed actions and the next step differ per stage. Which pattern cleanly models the changing behaviour?
optionB: State
optionC: Facade
correctAnswerText: State
rationale: Stage-dependent behaviour and transitions is the State pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class LightState { public: virtual void next(TrafficLight&) = 0; };  class Green : public LightState { void next(TrafficLight&) override; /* sets Yellow */ };  class TrafficLight { LightState* st; public: void setState(LightState* s) { st = s; } void change() { st->next(*this); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Observer
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: State
