rationale: A context delegating to a current-state object that performs the transition to the next state is the State pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An order object holds a current-state object; calling ship() forwards to the state, which performs the action and swaps the order to the next state. Is “State” correct?
optionB: No — State cannot change the object
optionC: Yes — but only with two states
optionD: Yes — delegating behaviour to a current-state object that performs transitions is exactly the State pattern
correctAnswerText: Yes — delegating behaviour to a current-state object that performs transitions is exactly the State pattern
rationale: Delegating to a current-state object that performs the action and the transition is the State pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The State pattern lets an object ___.
optionA: change its behaviour when its internal state changes, as though its class changed
optionB: broadcast every change to a list of dependents
optionC: capture and later restore its state
optionD: pass a request along a chain of handlers
correctAnswerText: change its behaviour when its internal state changes, as though its class changed
rationale: State lets an object alter its behaviour as its internal state changes. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Chain of Responsibility.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: How does the State pattern replace a sprawling switch on a status field?
optionA: By making the object a single shared instance
optionB: By copying the object once per status value
optionC: By representing each state as an object the host delegates to, with states able to switch the host to another state
optionD: By notifying observers whenever the status changes
correctAnswerText: By representing each state as an object the host delegates to, with states able to switch the host to another state
rationale: Each state becomes its own object encapsulating that mode’s behaviour and transitions; the host delegates to the current one, which can switch it.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A vending machine behaves differently when idle, coin-inserted, or dispensing, and each event can move it into another mode with its own allowed actions. Which approach fits?
optionA: Let the caller pick the behaviour from a menu and inject it once
optionB: Give each mode its own object the machine delegates to, with each mode triggering the move to the next
optionC: Notify a list of subscribers each time the machine changes
optionD: Wrap each machine event as an undoable command
correctAnswerText: Give each mode its own object the machine delegates to, with each mode triggering the move to the next
rationale: Mode-dependent behaviour where the mode objects themselves drive transitions is State. The distractors describe Strategy, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Strategy — an injected algorithm chosen by the caller
optionB: Observer — it notifies dependents
optionC: Command — a reified request
optionD: State — behaviour is delegated to a current-state object that performs the transition
correctAnswerText: State — behaviour is delegated to a current-state object that performs the transition
rationale: The host delegates to a current-state object that itself triggers the move to the next state — the hallmark of State, not caller-chosen Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Behaviour is delegated to a polymorphic object, and that object itself decides, based on incoming events, to replace the host’s current behaviour object — driving transitions. Is this State or Strategy?
optionA: State — the behaviour objects drive transitions based on the host’s evolving state
optionB: Strategy — any delegation to a polymorphic object is Strategy
optionC: Observer — it notifies dependents of changes
optionD: Template Method — it fixes an algorithm skeleton
correctAnswerText: State — the behaviour objects drive transitions based on the host’s evolving state
rationale: When the delegated objects themselves drive transitions based on the host’s changing state, it is State; Strategy is caller-chosen and does not self-transition.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Strategy pattern?
optionA: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms and make them swappable at runtime
optionB: Convert one interface into another
optionC: Ensure a single instance
correctAnswerText: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms and make them swappable at runtime
rationale: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does Strategy let the algorithm change without changing the client?
optionA: The client edits the algorithm’s code each time
optionB: The client depends on a strategy interface and delegates, so concrete algorithms swap freely
optionC: The client inherits each algorithm
optionD: The algorithms share one global instance
correctAnswerText: The client depends on a strategy interface and delegates, so concrete algorithms swap freely
rationale: The client holds a strategy interface and delegates; concrete strategies are interchangeable behind it.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A checkout must compute shipping cost by several methods (flat, weight-based, distance-based), chosen per order at runtime, without if/else sprawl. Which pattern?
optionB: Template Method
optionC: Strategy
correctAnswerText: Strategy
rationale: Selecting among interchangeable algorithms at runtime is Strategy. Template Method fixes a skeleton; State is driven by internal state; Observer notifies.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class SortStrategy { public: virtual void sort(std::vector<int>&) = 0; };  class Quick : public SortStrategy { void sort(std::vector<int>&) override; };  class Sorter { SortStrategy* s; public: Sorter(SortStrategy* x) : s(x) {} void run(std::vector<int>& v) { s->sort(v); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Command
optionC: Template Method
correctAnswerText: Strategy
rationale: A context delegating to an injected, interchangeable algorithm object is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A design swaps an object’s algorithm via an interface, but the object never changes which one it uses based on its own internal state — the client picks it. Is Strategy or State the better fit?
optionA: Strategy — it is a client-selected interchangeable algorithm, not state-driven behaviour
optionB: State — any swappable behaviour is State
optionC: Observer — it notifies dependents
correctAnswerText: Strategy — it is a client-selected interchangeable algorithm, not state-driven behaviour
rationale: Strategy is a client-chosen algorithm; State changes behaviour as the object’s own state transitions. Here the client selects it, so Strategy fits.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Strategy’s purpose is to ___.
optionB: make a set of algorithms interchangeable behind a common interface
optionC: build an object step by step
correctAnswerText: make a set of algorithms interchangeable behind a common interface
rationale: Strategy makes algorithms interchangeable behind one interface. The distractors are Observer, Builder, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What is the relationship between context and strategy?
optionA: The strategy owns and controls the context
optionB: Context and strategy are the same object
optionC: The context delegates the varying behaviour to a strategy object it holds
optionD: The strategy must be a Singleton
correctAnswerText: The context delegates the varying behaviour to a strategy object it holds
rationale: The context delegates the variable behaviour to a held strategy, so swapping strategies changes behaviour without touching the context.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A game character’s pathfinding should switch among A*, Dijkstra, and greedy at runtime depending on settings, with the rest of the code unchanged. Which pattern?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Visitor
correctAnswerText: Strategy
rationale: Interchangeable algorithms selected at runtime behind one interface is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Compressor { public: virtual void compress(File&) = 0; };  class Zip : public Compressor { void compress(File&) override; };  class Archiver { Compressor* c; public: Archiver(Compressor* x) : c(x) {} void store(File& f) { c->compress(f); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Observer
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Strategy
rationale: A context (Archiver) delegating to an injected interchangeable algorithm (Compressor) is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class accepts a payment-algorithm object implementing a common interface and delegates the calculation to it, letting callers pass any algorithm. Is “Strategy” a defensible label?
optionA: No — this must be Template Method
optionB: Yes — delegating interchangeable algorithms through a common interface chosen by the caller is exactly Strategy
optionC: No — Strategy requires inheriting the context
optionD: Yes — but only with exactly two algorithms
correctAnswerText: Yes — delegating interchangeable algorithms through a common interface chosen by the caller is exactly Strategy
rationale: Caller-chosen interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface, delegated by the context, is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A Strategy design groups several ___ behind one shared interface so the host can use any of them.
optionB: one-to-many notifications
optionC: state snapshots
correctAnswerText: interchangeable algorithms
rationale: Strategy bundles interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. The distractors describe Observer, Memento, and Interpreter.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In a Strategy design, why does the host class keep a reference to the strategy interface rather than a concrete algorithm?
optionA: So the strategy can notify the host of changes
optionB: So that any concrete algorithm can be plugged in and delegated to without editing the host
optionC: So the host can inherit each algorithm in turn
optionD: So all algorithms share a single global instance
correctAnswerText: So that any concrete algorithm can be plugged in and delegated to without editing the host
rationale: Holding the interface lets the host delegate to any concrete algorithm interchangeably, so swapping behaviour never touches the host.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A media exporter must encode video with H.264, VP9, or AV1, with the choice picked from a settings menu at runtime and the rest of the export pipeline untouched. Which approach fits?
optionA: Let an internal mode drive the encoder transitions
optionB: Notify a list of subscribers each time encoding finishes
optionC: Pull each encoder behind a shared interface and have the pipeline delegate to the one the caller selected
optionD: Wrap each encode request as a storable, undoable object
correctAnswerText: Pull each encoder behind a shared interface and have the pipeline delegate to the one the caller selected
rationale: Caller-selected, interchangeable algorithms behind one interface is Strategy. The distractors describe State, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: State — the rule changes the object’s mode
optionB: Template Method — a fixed skeleton with overridable steps
optionC: Command — the request is reified for undo
optionD: Strategy — the host delegates to an injected, interchangeable algorithm
correctAnswerText: Strategy — the host delegates to an injected, interchangeable algorithm
rationale: Invoice delegates the varying calculation to an injected TaxRule it never names concretely, with no state-transition methods — that is Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A class accepts a compression algorithm object through its constructor and delegates compression to it; the object never swaps itself out, and the caller decides which one to pass. Is labelling this Strategy defensible?
optionA: Yes — caller-chosen, interchangeable, delegated algorithms with no self-driven transitions is exactly Strategy
optionB: No — it must be State because behaviour is delegated to a polymorphic object
optionC: No — Strategy requires the host to inherit the algorithm
optionD: Yes — but only if there are at least three algorithms available
correctAnswerText: Yes — caller-chosen, interchangeable, delegated algorithms with no self-driven transitions is exactly Strategy
rationale: Strategy is a caller-selected algorithm injected and delegated to; the absence of self-driven state transitions distinguishes it from State.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Template Method pattern?
optionA: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses
optionB: Convert interfaces
optionC: Ensure one instance
correctAnswerText: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses
rationale: Template Method fixes an algorithm’s skeleton and lets subclasses supply steps. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Flyweight.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: In Template Method, what stays fixed and what varies?
optionB: The overall step sequence is fixed in the base class while specific steps are overridden by subclasses
optionC: Nothing can be overridden
optionD: The client supplies the whole algorithm at runtime
correctAnswerText: The overall step sequence is fixed in the base class while specific steps are overridden by subclasses
rationale: The invariant sequence lives in the base method; subclasses override only the designated variable steps.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Several report generators share the same overall flow (open → fetch → format → save) but differ only in how they format. You want the flow defined once. Which pattern?
optionB: Command
optionC: Template Method
correctAnswerText: Template Method
rationale: A fixed algorithm skeleton with a varying step implemented per subclass is Template Method. Strategy would inject the whole algorithm as an object.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Game { public: void play() { start(); while (!over()) turn(); end(); } protected: virtual void start() = 0; virtual void turn() = 0; virtual bool over() = 0; virtual void end() = 0; };  class Chess : public Game { /* overrides */ };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: State
optionC: Command
correctAnswerText: Template Method
rationale: A non-virtual base method fixing the flow and calling overridable steps is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An algorithm’s invariant steps live in a base method that calls overridable hooks; subclasses customise only those hooks. Is this Template Method or Strategy?
optionA: Template Method — variation happens via subclass overrides of fixed hook steps (inheritance), not a swapped strategy object
optionB: Strategy — any variation is Strategy
optionC: Observer — it notifies dependents
correctAnswerText: Template Method — variation happens via subclass overrides of fixed hook steps (inheritance), not a swapped strategy object
rationale: Template Method varies behaviour through subclass overrides of fixed hooks (inheritance); Strategy varies it by composing a swappable object.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Template Method defines ___.
optionB: an algorithm’s skeleton whose selected steps subclasses fill in without changing its structure
optionC: a surrogate for access control
correctAnswerText: an algorithm’s skeleton whose selected steps subclasses fill in without changing its structure
rationale: Template Method defines an algorithm skeleton with subclass-filled steps. The distractors are Observer, Proxy, and Composite.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Where is the invariant control flow located in Template Method?
optionB: In a separate strategy object
optionC: In a base-class method that calls the overridable primitive operations in a fixed order
correctAnswerText: In a base-class method that calls the overridable primitive operations in a fixed order
rationale: The base class owns the fixed control flow and calls the overridable steps in order.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Data importers for CSV, JSON, and XML all follow validate → parse → transform → persist, differing only in parsing. You want to write the sequence once. Which pattern?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Bridge
correctAnswerText: Template Method
rationale: A shared fixed sequence with one varying step per subclass is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Beverage { public: void make() { boil(); brew(); pour(); addExtras(); } protected: void boil(); void pour(); virtual void brew() = 0; virtual void addExtras() = 0; };  class Tea : public Beverage { /* overrides brew, addExtras */ };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Strategy
optionC: Decorator
correctAnswerText: Template Method
rationale: A fixed make() sequence calling some concrete and some overridable steps is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A base class fixes the sequence run() { setUp(); execute(); tearDown(); } where execute() is abstract and subclasses implement it. Is “Template Method” defensible?
optionB: Yes — a fixed skeleton with subclass-supplied steps via overriding is exactly Template Method
optionC: No — Template Method forbids abstract steps
optionD: Yes — but only with one subclass
correctAnswerText: Yes — a fixed skeleton with subclass-supplied steps via overriding is exactly Template Method
rationale: A fixed skeleton whose variable steps are supplied by subclass overrides is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: The Template Method pattern keeps an algorithm’s ___ fixed in a base method while letting subclasses supply certain steps.
optionB: overall step sequence
optionC: state-transition table
correctAnswerText: overall step sequence
rationale: Template Method fixes the overall step sequence in the base class and defers chosen steps to subclasses. The distractors belong to Observer, State, and Command.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: In a Template Method design, what is fixed and what varies?
optionA: Everything is overridden by each subclass
optionB: The base method fixes the order of steps while subclasses override only the designated variable steps
optionC: Nothing in the algorithm can ever be overridden
optionD: The caller injects the entire algorithm as a separate object
correctAnswerText: The base method fixes the order of steps while subclasses override only the designated variable steps
rationale: The invariant control flow lives in the base method; subclasses override only the marked variable steps, not the sequence.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Three exporters share the flow open → gather → render → close and differ only in how they render, and you want that flow written exactly once. Which approach fits?
optionA: Inject the whole export algorithm as a swappable object the caller chooses
optionB: Put the fixed flow in a base method that calls an overridable render step subclasses implement
optionC: Pass the export request through a chain of handlers
optionD: Coordinate the exporters through a central hub
correctAnswerText: Put the fixed flow in a base method that calls an overridable render step subclasses implement
rationale: A fixed skeleton in a base method calling an overridable step per subclass is Template Method; injecting the whole algorithm would be Strategy.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionA: Strategy — the body is an injected algorithm object
optionB: State — build changes with internal mode
optionC: Template Method — a fixed sequence calling an overridable hook step
correctAnswerText: Template Method — a fixed sequence calling an overridable hook step
rationale: A non-overridden base method fixing the order and calling an overridable hook (body) implemented by subclasses is Template Method.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An algorithm’s invariant steps live in a base method that invokes overridable hooks, and subclasses customise behaviour only by overriding those hooks via inheritance. Is this better called Template Method or Strategy?
optionA: Strategy — any variation in behaviour is Strategy
optionB: Observer — the hooks notify dependents
optionC: Composite — the steps form a tree
optionD: Template Method — variation comes from subclass overrides of fixed hook steps, not a composed swappable object
correctAnswerText: Template Method — variation comes from subclass overrides of fixed hook steps, not a composed swappable object
rationale: Template Method varies behaviour through inheritance and subclass overrides of fixed hooks; Strategy varies it by composing a swappable algorithm object.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What is the intent of the Visitor pattern?
optionA: Represent an operation to perform on the elements of an object structure, letting you add new operations without changing the element classes
optionB: Convert interfaces
optionC: Ensure one instance
correctAnswerText: Represent an operation to perform on the elements of an object structure, letting you add new operations without changing the element classes
rationale: Visitor adds operations over a structure without modifying its element classes. The distractors are Adapter, Singleton, and Iterator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: How does Visitor add a new operation across a class hierarchy without editing those classes?
optionB: Each element accepts a visitor and dispatches to the visitor’s method for its type; new operations are new visitor classes
optionC: By making each element a Singleton
correctAnswerText: Each element accepts a visitor and dispatches to the visitor’s method for its type; new operations are new visitor classes
rationale: Elements accept() a visitor and call back the type-specific visit() (double dispatch); a new operation is simply a new visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: An AST has many node types; you must add operations like type-check, pretty-print, and optimise without modifying each node class every time. Which pattern?
optionB: Iterator
optionC: Visitor
correctAnswerText: Visitor
rationale: Adding many operations over a stable element hierarchy without editing elements is Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class Visitor; class Node { public: virtual void accept(Visitor&) = 0; };  class Number : public Node { public: int v; void accept(Visitor& vi) override; /* vi.visit(*this) */ };  class Visitor { public: virtual void visit(Number&) = 0; virtual void visit(Add&) = 0; };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Mediator
optionC: Observer
correctAnswerText: Visitor
rationale: accept()/visit() double dispatch over an element hierarchy is the Visitor pattern.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A team needs to add many unrelated operations over a stable set of node types and rarely adds new node types. Is Visitor an appropriate choice, and why?
optionA: Yes — Visitor fits stable element types with frequently-added operations, localising each operation in a visitor (though adding new element types is costly)
optionB: No — Visitor is for adding new element types easily
optionC: No — Visitor cannot support multiple operations
optionD: Yes — because Visitor makes adding new node types trivial
correctAnswerText: Yes — Visitor fits stable element types with frequently-added operations, localising each operation in a visitor (though adding new element types is costly)
rationale: Visitor suits stable element hierarchies with many added operations; its known cost is that new element types require touching every visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: Visitor lets you ___.
optionB: add new operations over an object structure without modifying the structure’s classes
optionC: notify many observers
optionD: encapsulate a request as an object
correctAnswerText: add new operations over an object structure without modifying the structure’s classes
rationale: Visitor adds operations without modifying element classes. The distractors are Flyweight, Observer, and Command.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: What technique lets the correct visitor method run for each concrete element type?
optionA: A global switch on a type tag
optionB: Reflection at link time
optionC: Double dispatch — accept() on the element calls the visitor overload specific to that element’s type
correctAnswerText: Double dispatch — accept() on the element calls the visitor overload specific to that element’s type
rationale: Double dispatch (element.accept → visitor.visit overload) selects the right operation for the concrete element type.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A shape hierarchy needs new operations (area, export-to-SVG, bounding-box) added over time without touching each shape class for every new operation. Which pattern?
optionB: Bridge
optionC: State
correctAnswerText: Visitor
rationale: Adding operations over a stable hierarchy without editing element classes is Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Examine:  class ShapeVisitor { public: virtual void visit(Circle&) = 0; virtual void visit(Square&) = 0; };  class Shape { public: virtual void accept(ShapeVisitor&) = 0; };  class Circle : public Shape { void accept(ShapeVisitor& v) override { v.visit(*this); } };  Which pattern is this?
optionB: Composite
optionC: Strategy
correctAnswerText: Visitor
rationale: accept()/visit() double dispatch over a shape hierarchy is Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: Adding a new node type to a working Visitor design forces edits to every visitor class. Is this a known and acceptable trade-off of Visitor?
optionA: No — Visitor makes adding element types free
optionB: Yes — Visitor trades cheap new operations for costly new element types; this is its well-known trade-off, acceptable when element types are stable
optionC: No — it means the design is wrong
correctAnswerText: Yes — Visitor trades cheap new operations for costly new element types; this is its well-known trade-off, acceptable when element types are stable
rationale: Visitor’s recognised trade-off: easy new operations, costly new element types — acceptable when the element set is stable.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns; CodiNeo Behavioural module content

prompt: The Visitor pattern lets you ___.
optionA: add new operations over an object structure without modifying the structure’s element classes
optionB: walk a collection while hiding its storage
optionC: centralise communication between objects
optionD: restore an object to a saved state
correctAnswerText: add new operations over an object structure without modifying the structure’s element classes
rationale: Visitor adds operations over a structure without editing the element classes. The distractors describe Iterator, Mediator, and Memento.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What technique lets the correct operation run for each concrete element type in a Visitor design?
optionA: A global switch over an integer type tag
optionB: Reflection performed at link time
optionC: Double dispatch — each element’s accept calls back the visitor overload matching the element’s concrete type
optionD: Copying each element before operating on it
correctAnswerText: Double dispatch — each element’s accept calls back the visitor overload matching the element’s concrete type
rationale: Double dispatch (element.accept → the visitor’s type-specific visit) selects the right operation for each concrete element type; a new operation is just a new visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A document model has paragraph, image, and table nodes; you must keep adding operations like word-count, export-to-HTML, and spell-check without editing each node class for every new operation. Which approach fits?
optionA: Give each node a current-state object that changes its behaviour
optionB: Move each operation into a separate visitor with one visit method per node type, with nodes calling back via accept
optionC: Pass each node through a chain of handlers
optionD: Coordinate the nodes through a central hub
correctAnswerText: Move each operation into a separate visitor with one visit method per node type, with nodes calling back via accept
rationale: Adding many operations over a stable element hierarchy without editing the elements, via accept/visit double dispatch, is Visitor. The distractors describe State, Chain of Responsibility, and Mediator.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

optionB: Mediator — peers coordinated by a hub
optionC: Observer — a subject notifying dependents
optionD: Visitor — accept/visit double dispatch adding operations over an element hierarchy
correctAnswerText: Visitor — accept/visit double dispatch adding operations over an element hierarchy
rationale: Elements exposing accept and calling back a type-specific operation (double dispatch) so operations live in the operation object is Visitor.
sourceReferences: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: A team must add many unrelated operations over a node hierarchy whose set of node types almost never changes. Is Visitor an appropriate choice, and why?
optionA: Yes — Visitor suits a stable element set with frequently-added operations, isolating each operation in its own visitor (accepting that new element types would be costly)
optionB: No — Visitor is meant for adding new element types cheaply
optionC: No — Visitor can express only a single operation
optionD: Yes — because Visitor makes adding new node types trivial
correctAnswerText: Yes — Visitor suits a stable element set with frequently-added operations, isolating each operation in its own visitor (accepting that new element types would be costly)
rationale: Visitor fits stable element hierarchies with many added operations; its known cost is that introducing a new element type forces edits to every visitor.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Behavioural module content; Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994), Design Patterns

prompt: What does the PIMPL (pointer to implementation) idiom do?
optionB: Shares intrinsic state across many objects
optionC: Hides a class’s private members behind a pointer to an implementation struct, keeping the header stable
correctAnswerText: Hides a class’s private members behind a pointer to an implementation struct, keeping the header stable
rationale: PIMPL moves private members into a hidden implementation accessed through a pointer, so the header stays stable. The distractors are Adapter, Flyweight, and Observer.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); CodiNeo Idioms module content

prompt: How should PIMPL be classified relative to the GoF design patterns?
optionB: As a GoF structural pattern
optionC: As a GoF creational pattern
optionD: As a C++ implementation idiom (a compilation firewall), distinct from Bridge
correctAnswerText: As a C++ implementation idiom (a compilation firewall), distinct from Bridge
rationale: PIMPL is a C++ implementation idiom for header insulation, not a GoF pattern, and is distinct from Bridge (which varies an abstraction/implementation hierarchy).
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A widely-included library header keeps changing its private members, forcing slow recompiles of every client. You want the header to stop exposing those members so clients rarely recompile. Which technique fits?
optionA: The PIMPL idiom (pointer to a hidden implementation)
optionB: The Composite pattern
optionC: The Observer pattern
correctAnswerText: The PIMPL idiom (pointer to a hidden implementation)
rationale: Insulating clients from private-member changes via a hidden implementation pointer is PIMPL. The distractors solve unrelated problems.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)

prompt: Examine (widget.h):  class Widget { public: Widget(); ~Widget(); void draw(); private: struct Impl; std::unique_ptr<Impl> p; };  with all members defined in widget.cpp. Which C++ idiom does this header demonstrate?
optionB: PIMPL idiom
optionC: Flyweight pattern
correctAnswerText: PIMPL idiom
rationale: An opaque nested Impl held by a smart pointer, with definitions in the .cpp, is the PIMPL idiom (a compilation firewall).
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A developer says a class “uses Bridge” because it holds a unique_ptr<Impl>. But there is exactly one Impl type whose only purpose is to hide private members and reduce header dependencies — no abstraction/implementation hierarchy varies. Is “Bridge” accurate?
optionB: Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are interchangeable terms
optionC: No — a single fixed implementation used only to hide members and stabilise the header is the PIMPL idiom, not Bridge
optionD: No — this is actually the Adapter pattern
correctAnswerText: No — a single fixed implementation used only to hide members and stabilise the header is the PIMPL idiom, not Bridge
rationale: With one fixed implementation used solely for header insulation, this is PIMPL, not Bridge — Bridge requires independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: The PIMPL idiom primarily ___.
optionB: defines interchangeable algorithms
optionC: captures and restores object state
optionD: moves a class’s private implementation behind a pointer so the header stays stable and dependencies shrink
correctAnswerText: moves a class’s private implementation behind a pointer so the header stays stable and dependencies shrink
rationale: PIMPL relocates the private implementation behind a pointer to insulate the header and cut dependencies. The distractors are Flyweight, Strategy, and Memento.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); CodiNeo Idioms module content

prompt: Which benefits does PIMPL provide?
optionA: Reduced header/compile-time dependencies, a more stable header/ABI, and hidden implementation details
optionB: Runtime algorithm swapping
optionC: Automatic object pooling
correctAnswerText: Reduced header/compile-time dependencies, a more stable header/ABI, and hidden implementation details
rationale: PIMPL’s benefits are fewer header dependencies, a more stable header/ABI, and hidden implementation details. The distractors describe Strategy, Flyweight, and Observer.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A team wants to change a class’s private data members in future releases without forcing every client translation unit that includes the header to recompile. Which technique achieves this insulation?
optionB: The PIMPL idiom
optionC: Decorator
correctAnswerText: The PIMPL idiom
rationale: Hiding private members behind an implementation pointer so header changes (and recompiles) are avoided is PIMPL.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)

prompt: Examine (engine.h):  class Engine { public: Engine(); ~Engine(); void run(); private: class Impl; Impl* impl_; };  with Impl defined only in engine.cpp. This header pattern is which idiom?
optionB: Adapter pattern
optionC: PIMPL idiom
correctAnswerText: PIMPL idiom
rationale: An opaque nested Impl referenced by pointer and defined only in the .cpp is the PIMPL idiom.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A class exposes only public methods in its header and forwards every call to a privately-pointed Impl defined in the source file, with one Impl type whose sole job is hiding members. Is calling this “PIMPL” (not Bridge) correct?
optionA: No — it must be called Bridge
optionB: No — PIMPL requires multiple implementations
optionC: Yes — but only if it is in the standard library
optionD: Yes — a single hidden implementation behind a pointer to insulate the header is PIMPL, distinct from Bridge’s varying abstraction/implementation hierarchies
correctAnswerText: Yes — a single hidden implementation behind a pointer to insulate the header is PIMPL, distinct from Bridge’s varying abstraction/implementation hierarchies
rationale: A single hidden implementation behind a pointer used to insulate the header is PIMPL; Bridge would require independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies.
sourceReferences: Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL); Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: What does the PIMPL idiom move out of a class’s public header?
optionA: Its private members, into a hidden implementation type reached through a pointer
optionB: Its public methods, into a base class
optionC: Its constructor, into a factory
correctAnswerText: Its private members, into a hidden implementation type reached through a pointer
rationale: PIMPL relocates private members into a hidden Impl type accessed via a pointer, keeping the header stable.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content; Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)

prompt: Why does PIMPL reduce client recompilation when private members change?
optionA: Because clients stop including any headers
optionB: Because PIMPL disables the preprocessor
optionC: Because the compiler caches everything forever
optionD: Because the private members live in the .cpp, so changing them does not change the header clients include
correctAnswerText: Because the private members live in the .cpp, so changing them does not change the header clients include
rationale: With private state hidden in the implementation file, header-included clients are insulated from those changes and need not recompile.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content; Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)

prompt: A heavily-included header keeps changing its private fields, forcing slow full rebuilds. Which technique stabilises the header so clients rarely recompile?
optionB: Add more public getters
optionC: Hide the private members behind a pointer to an implementation defined in the .cpp
optionD: Convert the class to a template
correctAnswerText: Hide the private members behind a pointer to an implementation defined in the .cpp
rationale: Hiding private members behind an implementation pointer (PIMPL) stabilises the header and cuts client recompiles.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content; Sutter (1999), Exceptional C++ — Compilation Firewalls (PIMPL)

prompt: A header declares: class Widget { public: Widget(); ~Widget(); void draw(); private: struct Impl; std::unique_ptr<Impl> p; };  with Impl defined only in widget.cpp. Which technique is shown?
optionB: The PIMPL idiom (an opaque Impl owned via a pointer)
optionC: Flyweight pattern
correctAnswerText: The PIMPL idiom (an opaque Impl owned via a pointer)
rationale: A forward-declared Impl owned through a smart pointer, defined in the .cpp, is the PIMPL idiom.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++

prompt: A class holds one unique_ptr<Impl> whose sole job is hiding private members and stabilising the header — no abstraction varies. Is calling it “Bridge” accurate?
optionB: No — this is actually Adapter
optionC: Yes — Bridge and PIMPL are the same thing
optionD: No — a single fixed implementation used only to insulate the header is PIMPL; Bridge needs independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
correctAnswerText: No — a single fixed implementation used only to insulate the header is PIMPL; Bridge needs independently varying abstraction and implementation hierarchies
rationale: One fixed Impl for header insulation is PIMPL, not Bridge; Bridge requires an abstraction and an independently varying implementation hierarchy.
sourceReferences: CodiNeo Idioms module content; Nesteruk (2022), Design Patterns in Modern C++
